name: Ideation & Brainstorming
description: The designer provides a design challenge, problem statement, or brief and works iteratively with an LLM to generate candidate concepts, directions, and ideas across a broad solution space. AI produces rapid divergent output; the designer evaluates, selects, and develops promising directions. The value is in expanding ideation breadth early in the concept phase - surfacing directions the designer might not have considered - while compressing the time from problem framing to initial concept shortlist. A secondary pattern is cold-start facilitation: when the practitioner does not yet know the domain well enough to begin, AI-generated directions provide scaffolding that makes the first iteration possible.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: evaluating which AI-generated concepts have genuine design potential vs. superficial novelty, and developing selected directions into coherent approaches that hold up against user needs and constraints
grounding concept selection in evidence, ensuring creatively interesting directions also address the problem the research identified rather than solving a different or more tractable problem

value_hypothesis.sentence: Expands the solution space rapidly at the start of concept development, surfacing a wider range of directions than solo or small-group brainstorming while reducing the friction of blank-page ideation.

value_indicator.description: number of distinct concept directions generated per session relative to solo brainstorming baseline
elapsed time to initial concept shortlist relative to team workshop baseline

risk_typology.narrative: AI generates concepts that cluster around familiar design patterns and well-represented solution archetypes, narrowing the effective solution space toward conventional directions rather than genuinely expanding it; practitioner evidence suggests AI output is reliably useful for first-pass breadth but consistently falls short of the distinctive directions that define strong concept work
fluently described AI concepts may appear more resolved than they are, creating premature closure around directions not yet evaluated against user needs or constraints
habitual reliance on AI for divergent ideation may atrophy the designer's own generative capacity and intuition for what constitutes a strong concept direction

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - AI-assisted ideation is widely practised and positively received by designers, but has not yet been formally integrated into standard design workflows or evaluated against controlled baselines in naturalistic settings.

name: Wireframe & Layout Generation
description: The designer specifies page objectives, content requirements, or user flows, and an AI tool proposes structure and hierarchy, potential layouts, and component hierarchies. The designer evaluates proposed layouts critically - testing them against user goals, business requirements, interaction logic, and design system constraints - selecting, adapting, or rejecting AI proposals. The Challenger arrangement reflects the asymmetry of the UC: AI produces candidate layouts with structural specificity, and the designer's primary contribution is interrogating those proposals rather than generating from scratch.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: evaluating whether AI-proposed layouts correctly prioritise content, support the intended user flow, and reflect hierarchy decisions grounded in user needs rather than visual convention
assessing layout proposals against responsive behaviour requirements, component library limitations, and implementation constraints invisible to the AI but immediately consequential for development

value_hypothesis.sentence: Accelerates the transition from content requirements to evaluated layout structures, generating candidate wireframes for designer critique rather than requiring manual construction of each layout option.

value_indicator.description: elapsed time from content specification to evaluated wireframe shortlist relative to manual wireframing baseline
proportion of AI-generated layout proposals conforming to design system constraints without requiring manual correction

risk_typology.narrative: AI layout generation converges on familiar grid patterns, standard UI conventions, and over-represented interface archetypes, producing layouts that are structurally adequate but architecturally undistinctive

AI-generated wireframes may appear sufficiently resolved to be mistaken for deliberate design decisions, suppressing critical examination of layout logic before visual design begins; at multi-screen scale, individual screens may pass review while the collection fails on consistency - style drift, missing contextual carry-forward, and layout artefacts that only become visible when screens are read in sequence
proposed layouts may ignore responsive behaviour requirements, component library limitations, or implementation constraints invisible to the AI but immediately consequential for development

attestation.diagnostic: Adoption gap - AI wireframing tools are commercially available and actively marketed, but structured designer evaluation protocols before advancing AI-generated layouts have not yet become standard practice.

name: Prompt-to-Prototype and Vibe Coding
description: The designer describes interaction behaviors, user flows, or screen states in natural language, and an AI coding assistant generates functional prototype code. Designer and AI alternate: the designer evaluates the result in browser or preview, identifies gaps or refinements, and re-prompts. Unlike static wireframing, this approach produces interactive, clickable prototypes suitable for stakeholder review and user testing without requiring engineering resource. The workflow also extends to late-stage frontend polish - refining visual detail directly in a code editor - where practitioners report a material reduction in implementation work as a share of total time. The Co-creator arrangement reflects genuine back-and-forth: substantive creative and structural decisions made by the designer shape what the AI builds next.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: specifying interaction behaviors, transition logic, edge cases, and error states with the precision needed to prompt AI effectively and evaluate whether AI-generated interactions match design intent

selecting which interactions are worth prototyping, calibrating prototype scope against the stakeholder questions that need answering, and preventing extensive vibe coding that tests the wrong hypotheses

assessing whether AI-generated prototype code is suitable for testing purposes vs. production use, and recognising when vibe-coded implementations establish interaction patterns that would be impractical to replicate at scale

value_hypothesis.sentence: Enables designers to produce functional, interactive prototypes without engineering support, unlocking prototype fidelity levels previously gated by development resource availability.

value_indicator.description: whether the use case enables a designer to produce functional code-based prototypes without engineering support - a workflow not previously accessible at this fidelity level

elapsed time from interaction specification to testable prototype relative to engineering-supported prototype baseline; practitioner evidence also points to a reduction in implementation time as a proportion of the overall design workflow

risk_typology.narrative: vibe-coded prototypes are built from code the designer cannot read, interrogate, or explain; when interactions break or need adaptation, there is no basis for diagnosis, and engineering teams inheriting the prototype inherit implementation decisions no one can account for

vibe-coded prototypes establish interaction expectations and visual fidelity that may be difficult or impossible to replicate in production, creating a gap between what was tested and what can be built

habitual vibe coding without understanding underlying implementation may reduce the designer's ability to specify interactions with the precision engineering teams require, and erode awareness of technical constraints that shape design decisions

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - code-generation tools are capable and widely trialled, but integrating vibe-coded prototypes into design workflows - including handoff conventions, testing protocols, and developer review - has not yet been standardised.

name: Design Variants
description: The designer specifies design parameters - color palette, typography, layout direction, or component style - and an AI tool generates multiple visual variants simultaneously. The designer reviews the variant set, identifies which directions meet brand, accessibility, and design system requirements, and selects options for further development or stakeholder review. Approver arrangement: AI produces the variant array autonomously; the designer's role is gating - curating and advancing rather than co-generating. Unlike AI coding tools, which commit to a single implementation direction, design-tool-based variant generation supports non-linear exploration - enabling comparison across fundamentally different directions before any commitment is made.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: evaluating which AI-generated variants have genuine aesthetic merit, brand coherence, and emotional resonance vs. those that are technically compliant but visually undistinctive
assessing variants against token constraints, component standards, and scalability requirements before advancing any direction to development

value_hypothesis.sentence: Compresses the time required to explore a design space by generating multiple parallel variants simultaneously, enabling evaluation of more options than sequential manual exploration allows.

value_indicator.description: number of distinct design variants evaluable in a fixed time window relative to manual exploration baseline
whether the use case enables evaluation of variant sets at a scale not feasible through manual production

risk_typology.narrative: AI-generated variants cluster around statistically common design patterns, creating sets that appear diverse on the surface but share underlying compositional logic drawn from training data
reviewing a large variant set creates an appearance of thorough design exploration; if the variants share the same underlying logic, no real exploration of the design space has occurred
curation replacing generation as the primary design act may gradually narrow the designer's independent capacity to explore and compose visual directions

attestation.diagnostic: Adoption gap - AI variant generation is available within major design platforms, but structured evaluation criteria for selecting from AI-generated variant sets have not become standard practice.

name: Placeholder and First Draft Content Generation
description: During wireframing and prototyping, the designer prompts AI to generate two categories of content: placeholder imagery to populate layout compositions, and first-draft UX copy - microcopy, labels, error messages, onboarding text - to test messaging and information architecture before final content design. AI generates at volume; the designer reviews for contextual fit, demographic appropriateness, and tonal alignment, then gates what enters the prototype. This is content produced to test, not to ship - the distinction from AI-assisted copy generation in the Deliver phase (UC DEL-044), where content is refined for production. This UC is expected to evolve as two conditions mature: design systems become more deeply integrated into AI generation workflows, and those design systems incorporate content design guidance - voice principles, vocabulary constraints, tone frameworks. When both conditions are met, AI-generated first-draft content could become sufficiently scaffolded to reduce the Approver review burden and shift the arrangement toward Co-creator.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: evaluating whether AI-generated placeholder and first-draft copy is tonally and structurally appropriate for the design context, and ensuring it does not inadvertently set expectations about final content treatment or foreclose content design decisions
assessing AI-generated placeholder images for compositional fit, visual tone, and demographic representation appropriate to the product and its users

value_hypothesis.sentence: Populates design mockups with contextually appropriate placeholder imagery and first-draft copy faster than manual approaches, producing higher-fidelity prototypes that support more realistic stakeholder review and user testing.

value_indicator.description: time to populate a prototype with contextually relevant content relative to manual or Lorem ipsum baseline
assessed realism and contextual fit of AI-generated content vs. Lorem ipsum or manual first-draft baseline

risk_typology.narrative: AI-generated content may be polished enough to be mistaken for final approved copy or imagery, creating false confidence that content decisions have been made; stakeholders may approve a prototype based on placeholder material they believe is final

AI-generated placeholder images default to demographically unrepresentative or stereotyped depictions, and first-draft copy may reflect dominant-culture assumptions about user language, register, and mental models, embedding unexamined assumptions into prototypes that persist into final designs

AI first-draft copy hits the statistically correct tone register but misses the emotional texture specific to the product context and its users; error messages, onboarding flows, and microcopy that feel generically appropriate may fail to meet users where they actually are

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - AI-generated placeholder content is widely used informally, but protocols for distinguishing it from final approved content, managing bias in placeholder imagery, and governing first-draft copy in testing contexts have not been standardised.

name: Prompt Templates and Instruction File Development
description: The designer builds organisational AI infrastructure - instruction files, system prompts, and prompt templates that standardise how team members interact with AI tools. This shifts quality management from reactive to preventive: teams encode design system rules, heuristics, and quality standards into the generation context before work begins, rather than correcting AI output after the fact. Director arrangement: the human sets the constraints the instructions must encode; AI drafts and iterates but cannot supply organisational reasoning. Output is not a project artifact but a reusable organisational asset that governs future AI-assisted work.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: structuring instruction files with the same architectural discipline as design system components: composable, reusable, maintainable, and consistent across different team members' workflows
defining which organisational constraints, quality standards, and brand requirements need to be encoded, and prioritising which workflows benefit most from standardisation

value_hypothesis.sentence: Builds organisational AI capability through reusable prompt templates and instruction files that reduce variance in AI output quality across team members and compress the time practitioners spend learning to prompt effectively.

value_indicator.description: whether the use case produces reusable artifacts - templates, prompt libraries, instruction files - that accumulate as durable team capability
rate at which AI-enabled practices spread across the team once standardised templates are in place

risk_typology.narrative: instruction files may appear to standardise AI practice while encoding shallow or misaligned guidance; teams may believe they have governance infrastructure when the templates are too generic to materially improve output quality

instruction files created without documented reasoning become organisational dependencies no one can explain or adapt when AI tools change; the rationale for specific constraints is lost before anyone thinks to record it

over-reliance on pre-built templates may reduce practitioners' ability to craft context-specific prompts when templates don't fit, and may constrain the range of AI applications the team is willing to explore

attestation.diagnostic: Adoption gap - deliberate design of organisational AI infrastructure (instruction files, prompt libraries) is documented at innovator teams but has not yet become standard practice in design organisations.

name: Personalized Research Stimulus Generation
description: The researcher designs a stimulus framework for participant profiles, then works with AI to generate personalized versions of the materials shown during research - mockups, scenarios, vignettes, or concept descriptions - tailored to each participant's profile, role, use case, or proficiency level. Novice users get simpler interfaces with guidance; expert users see more complex versions with advanced features. Stimuli can be adapted across markets or segments, offering imagery, scenarios, and interaction patterns to fit expectations. The researcher evaluates whether AI-generated stimuli are realistic, non-leading, and appropriately scoped; AI generates and adapts content at scale. An extension of this use case is the "real-time hybrid session", where personalized concept variants are generated mid-interview based on what a participant has described - enabling simultaneous validation and directional feedback in a single session. This spans two phases: stimuli are designed during Concept and deployed during Validate.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: designing stimulus frameworks that produce authentic participant engagement, evaluating whether AI- generated materials will feel realistic to each participant, and ensuring personalisation does not introduce leading or confounding elements that compromise finding integrity
assessing whether personalized stimuli will activate the motivational and cognitive states relevant to the research questions, rather than merely matching surface attributes of participant profiles

value_hypothesis.sentence: Removes the constraint of "we can only test what we can build" by enabling personalised stimuli tailored to each participant's role, industry, proficiency level, or market without proportional increases in design or engineering time.

value_indicator.description: whether the use case enables stimulus personalisation across participant volumes, proficiency levels, or markets not feasible through manual production
assessed realism and contextual fit of AI-generated stimuli vs. generic stimuli baseline as rated by participants or independent reviewers

risk_typology.narrative: AI personalises stimuli based on stereotyped assumptions about roles, industries, or demographics, producing materials that feel inauthentic to participants or systematically misrepresent the lived situations of certain groups

AI-generated scenarios may appear realistic and contextually appropriate while embedding assumptions about participant contexts that participants would not recognise as their own, or that skew the responses elicited

stimuli personalised to surface-level attributes may miss the emotional texture or institutional complexity of participants' actual situations, reducing the authenticity of interaction the approach is intended to produce

attestation.diagnostic: Tool gap - context-aware stimulus personalisation at scale requires AI capabilities that exist but have not been packaged into research workflow tools; practice is at early experimenter stage, with real-time hybrid session generation in particular at proof-of-concept only.

name: Custom Figma Plugin Development
description: The designer identifies a repetitive or specialised task in their Figma workflow that no existing plugin addresses, specifies the desired functionality in natural language, and uses an AI coding assistant to generate plugin code. The designer tests the output, identifies gaps or errors, and re-prompts to refine. Unlike professional plugin development requiring JavaScript expertise, this approach makes custom workflow tooling accessible to designers without an engineering background. Director arrangement: the designer holds the workflow context and requirements the plugin must serve; AI generates the implementation without the designer needing to understand the underlying code. Output is not a design artifact but a persistent workflow tool - a reusable automation that extends beyond any single project. This UC spans two phases: the plugin is specified and built during Concept; it is deployed and maintained during Deliver.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: specifying plugin functionality that maps to real workflow bottlenecks, integrates correctly with design system structures, and is scoped to scale across different use cases within the team's tooling environment

assessing whether AI-generated plugin code will behave correctly within Figma's API constraints, and recognising implementation approaches likely to be brittle or difficult to maintain across Figma updates

value_hypothesis.sentence: Enables designers to build bespoke workflow automation without engineering support, creating custom tooling for repetitive or specialised tasks not covered by existing plugins.

value_indicator.description: proportion of target task steps handled by the plugin without manual intervention
whether the use case enables workflow automation not previously accessible to the design team without engineering support

risk_typology.narrative: vibe-coded plugins are built from code the designer cannot read or explain; when plugins break or behave unexpectedly, there is no basis for diagnosis, and maintenance depends on re-prompting rather than understanding
AI-generated plugin code may operate correctly in testing but fail under Figma API updates, edge-case file structures, or team-scale deployment conditions the designer could not anticipate
a functional plugin may appear to automate a workflow while creating a dependency on fragile code that breaks silently, replacing a manual inefficiency with a digital one that is harder to detect

attestation.diagnostic: Adoption gap - vibe-coded plugin development is documented at innovator level but requires both workflow sophistication and AI coding tool familiarity; practice has not spread to general design teams.

name: Moodboarding and Visual Language Exploration
description: The designer prompts an image generation model to produce visual references, moodboard components, and aesthetic explorations during the concept phase. Designer and AI iterate: the designer refines prompts, evaluates outputs for brand fit and directional validity, and selects images that articulate visual language directions to team members and stakeholders. Unlike stock photography or manual illustration, AI image generation enables exploration of highly specific visual combinations not available in reference libraries. The Co-creator arrangement reflects the iterative dynamic: aesthetic judgments made by the designer shape subsequent prompts; AI generates; the designer selects and redirects.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: evaluating AI-generated images for genuine aesthetic coherence, brand alignment, and directional validity rather than surface-level appeal; translating visual selections into defensible visual language rationale
grounding visual exploration in user and market context, ensuring aesthetic directions connect to the problem the research identified rather than reflecting designer preference alone

value_hypothesis.sentence: Enables exploration of visual language directions at a specificity and volume not achievable through stock photography or manual illustration, compressing the time from brief to visual direction shortlist.

value_indicator.description: whether the use case enables exploration of visual combinations not available in reference libraries or stock photography
number of distinct visual language directions evaluable in a fixed time window relative to manual moodboarding baseline

risk_typology.narrative: AI image generation converges on trending aesthetic archetypes; what appears to be visual exploration produces outputs that share the same underlying visual language across organisations using the same models and prompting conventions

AI-generated images may contain semantic anomalies - physically impossible elements, cultural misrepresentations - that pass casual review but undermine the credibility of visual directions when scrutinised

image generation models encode demographic and cultural biases in how they depict people, environments, and products, embedding assumptions into visual language directions that can persist into brand and design system decisions

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - AI image generation for visual exploration is widely practised informally but has not been integrated into formal visual direction processes with defined evaluation criteria or brand governance checkpoints.

name: System-Grounded Drafting
description: The designer connects an AI generation tool to the design system via a context protocol (e.g., MCP), enabling the AI to query available components, tokens, spacing rules, and brand constraints before generating artifacts. This connection is the enabling move: without it, AI generates from generic training data and every output requires manual correction against the system. With it, the AI drafts layouts, component compositions, and design explorations that start closer to system-compliant, compressing the gap between first draft and usable artifact. Director arrangement: the designer establishes the system connection and defines generation parameters; AI generates outputs the designer evaluates for genuine system conformance versus surface resemblance. The output is exploratory - concept-phase drafts that accelerate design exploration, not production-ready code. For production- scale generation with deterministic output and near- zero hallucination rates, see DEL-052.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: configuring the AI-design system connection correctly, evaluating whether AI-generated outputs genuinely conform to system constraints vs. superficially resembling system components, and maintaining system integrity as AI generation scales

assessing the integration architecture between AI tools and design system infrastructure, and understanding the limits of context-protocol-based provision: what the AI can and cannot reliably know about the system at generation time

value_hypothesis.sentence: Reduces the gap between AI-generated artifacts and production-ready outputs by grounding generation in the team's actual design system, compressing the manual correction cycle that standard AI generation requires.

value_indicator.description: proportion of AI-generated outputs conforming to design system constraints without manual correction, relative to non-system-aware generation baseline
reduction in manual correction time required to bring AI-generated artifacts into design system conformance

risk_typology.narrative: system-grounded generation may produce outputs that appear correctly system-compliant while containing subtle token mismatches, component misuse, or constraint violations that only surface at engineering handoff; practitioner evidence confirms that even with system context provided, AI does not reliably pick up all design system elements, requiring manual correction or designer-mediated workaround

when AI generates within a design system context, the rationale for specific component choices or layout decisions is attributed to the system rather than explained; design decisions become harder to interrogate and defend in review

design system context provided to AI may be incomplete or stale - outdated tokens, deprecated components - causing AI to generate confidently within constraints that no longer reflect the production system

attestation.diagnostic: Adoption gap - system-grounded generation is demonstrated by practitioner teams with strong design system foundations, but the configuration requirements and system hygiene prerequisites mean the capability is not yet accessible to most design organisations.

name: Scenario Generation
description: The designer provides a design challenge, persona sketch, or contextual brief, and works with AI to generate narrative design scenarios - stories describing how a user might interact with a proposed design in a real-world context. Scenarios help the team evaluate whether a concept holds up under realistic use conditions and communicate design intent to stakeholders. The Co-creator arrangement reflects iterative back-and-forth: the designer provides contextual grounding and evaluates whether scenarios are realistic, appropriately scoped, and generative for design decisions; AI drafts and elaborates narrative content at speed.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: evaluating whether AI-generated scenarios reflect realistic user motivations, situational constraints, and behavioral patterns rather than idealised interactions that would not arise in actual use
grounding scenario generation in evidence from prior research phases, ensuring scenarios connect to actual user needs and contexts rather than plausible-sounding but invented situations

value_hypothesis.sentence: Accelerates scenario generation during concept development, enabling teams to evaluate design directions against a wider range of use contexts than manual authoring allows.

value_indicator.description: time to scenario set from brief relative to manual authoring baseline
assessed realism and generative value of AI-generated scenarios vs. manually authored baseline

risk_typology.narrative: AI-generated scenarios may be structurally plausible but emotionally and contextually thin, missing the institutional pressures, social dynamics, and situational complexity that make scenarios genuinely useful for design evaluation

scenarios may appear realistic and well-grounded while encoding assumptions about user behavior that research has not established, producing false confidence in design directions not yet properly validated
AI scenario generation gravitates toward representative average users in typical situations, underrepresenting edge cases, marginalised users, and unusual contexts where design most commonly fails

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - AI-assisted scenario generation is practised by individual innovators but has not been integrated into formal design methodology; no established evaluation criteria for AI-generated scenarios exist.

name: Concept Critique
description: The designer presents a design concept - wireframes, mockups, or narrative descriptions - and prompts AI to evaluate it against specified criteria: user needs, design principles, consistency requirements, or competitive benchmarks. AI produces a structured critique; the designer evaluates each finding, accepting valid challenges, redirecting misapplied ones, and using the assessment as a prompt for design refinement. Challenger arrangement: AI proposes critical observations about the concept; the designer interrogates those observations rather than producing self-critique.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: evaluating whether AI critiques are grounded in genuine user evidence vs. generic design principles, and distinguishing substantive design problems from cosmetic or preference-based observations
assessing which AI-identified issues are consequential for user outcomes and business goals vs. which are technically valid but strategically immaterial

value_hypothesis.sentence: Provides structured external critique of design concepts at low cost, surfacing issues the designer may be too close to identify while compressing time to design review feedback.

value_indicator.description: proportion of AI-identified critique points subsequently validated by human design review
time from design concept to structured critique relative to peer review or design critique session baseline

risk_typology.narrative: AI assessment may identify issues with apparent rigor and specificity while missing the most consequential problems, or flagging non-problems; a structured-looking critique creates false confidence that the design has been thoroughly evaluated
completing an AI design assessment may be mistaken for genuine design review; teams may advance concepts based on AI critique without the depth of evaluation that expert human review provides
habitual use of AI-generated critiques may weaken the designer's own critical evaluation capacity and reduce the quality of independent design self-assessment over time

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - AI-assisted design critique is practised individually but structured evaluation protocols for AI-generated design assessment have not been established.

name: Design Remix
description: The designer supplies visual reference material - existing design concepts, style samples, layout variations, or idea sketches - and prompts AI to manipulate and fuse them to meet specific design requirements. AI generates new creative approaches from the inputs; the output is rough by design, a starting point rather than a resolved proposal. The designer keeps what matches their intent, discards the rest, and feeds refined selections or reframed elements back into the loop.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: distinguishing AI-fused concepts that produce meaningful synthesis from those that blend surface qualities without preserving design intent; directing the remix toward combinations that serve specific conceptual goals rather than arbitrary visual novelty

assessing whether structural and compositional logic from source concepts survives the fusion intact, rather than accepting outputs that appear visually coherent while embedding spatial or hierarchical contradictions

value_hypothesis.sentence: Enables designers to generate directionally new concepts by systematically recombining elements from disparate existing designs, expanding the explorable solution space beyond what manual iteration can reach in equivalent time.

value_indicator.description: whether AI-fused concepts produce directional combinations meaningfully distinct from any single source input
number of concept remix directions explored per session relative to manual recombination baseline

risk_typology.narrative: AI fusion gravitates toward the statistical midpoint of source inputs, neutralizing the contrasts that made the combination generative; the output smooths over distinctive features rather than synthesizing them productively, producing blends more generic than any individual source

systematic delegation of concept recombination to AI may degrade the designer's own capacity for combinatorial thinking and independent synthesis, reducing the conceptual judgment needed to direct the process well

fused concepts may appear aesthetically resolved while combining elements that are logically or functionally incompatible; visual coherence between merged components masks underlying structural incoherence

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - multimodal AI capable of image-based concept synthesis exists, but structured approaches for directing, evaluating, and integrating AI-fused concepts into design workflows have not been established; practice is individual and undocumented.

name: Research Preview
description: The team generates a functional product build before finalising visual design, and gets it in front of real users as early as possible - through a limited pilot, an internal preview, or a framed early release - to test functionality before investing in polish. Being explicit about the work's early state, and committing visibly to iterate on what users report, allows participants to engage as collaborators rather than judges of a finished product. The approach inverts the traditional design-then-build sequence: functionality is validated first, visual refinement follows. AI- assisted prototyping makes the inversion economically viable - a working build takes hours rather than weeks to generate, and the iteration loop is tight enough that feedback translates quickly into shipped improvements.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: deciding what is ready enough to share as an early build; calibrating the acceptable gap between current quality and organisational standards; and making - and visibly keeping - the commitment to iterate based on user feedback

distinguishing feedback on core functionality from feedback on visual quality or novelty; understanding which user responses represent real experience problems to address now vs. expectations that will be resolved by later polish

value_hypothesis.sentence: Compresses the concept-to-user-feedback cycle by shipping functional builds before visual polish, enabling iteration on real user behaviour while building product trust through demonstrated responsiveness rather than upfront quality.

value_indicator.description: elapsed time from concept to first user feedback on deployed functionality relative to traditional design-then-build baseline
whether the workflow enables a validation cadence not previously feasible within typical design and engineering resource constraints

risk_typology.narrative: validating that a feature works may be mistaken for validating the full user experience; teams may advance through development cycles with experience problems that only become visible when visual quality arrives, by which point they are expensive to address

early preview participants are often self-selected, internally recruited, or otherwise atypical; their feedback systematically underrepresents users with lower digital confidence, higher friction tolerance thresholds, or different mental models of what the product should do

systematic deferral of visual design to post-validation stages may gradually reduce practitioners' capacity for holistic experience reasoning; the discipline of designing before building encodes principles that feature-first approaches risk skipping

attestation.diagnostic: Trust gap - the methodology is practised and evidenced by innovator teams, but resistance persists in organisations where design-before-build norms, stakeholder expectations around fidelity, and governance processes are built around seeing polished visual design before any user exposure.

name: Cross-functional First Drafts
description: Non-designers - product managers, researchers, and engineers - use prompt-to-UI or -prototype as a "first draft" communication tool for expressing intent, but not as a design output. PMs include these mock-ups in PRDs to reduce requirement ambiguity; researchers to communicate research proposals to stakeholders; engineers scope implementation before coding begins. The value is shared understanding across functions, not a design artifact intended to progress into production. Co-creator arrangement: the non-designer stakeholder supplies intent and context; AI generates visual form until the communication purpose is served.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: recognising when a non-designer generated UI artifact is being treated as a design specification; understanding the distinction between visual communication aids and fully-reasoned design artifacts, and intervening before premature output enters the design pipeline as de facto specs

establishing team conventions that first-draft outputs from non-designers should be considered as communication aids rather than design commitments; determining when generated output has sufficient fidelity for its communication purpose without crossing into premature design commitment

value_hypothesis.sentence: Gives non-designer roles a visual communication channel for product intent that previously required design tool proficiency; reduces requirement ambiguity in PRDs, research proposals, and implementation scoping by replacing text descriptions with generated screens.

value_indicator.description: self-reported reduction in requirement ambiguity or misalignment relative to text-only communication baseline
whether non-designer roles gain a visual communication channel previously accessible only to practitioners with design tool proficiency

risk_typology.narrative: non-designer generated UI enters shared channels and is treated as a design artifact before design has been involved; visual polish signals design commitment that has not been made, shaping stakeholder expectations and constraining downstream design work before it has begun
generated screens reflect the non-designer author's mental model of UI rather than validated UX design; downstream teams may accept this as design intent when it is communication scaffolding

non-designer generated UI carries role-specific assumptions (PM: conversion priorities; developer: implementation simplicity) rather than user-centred design principles, embedding these biases into shared mental models before design input

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - cross-functional GenUI use for communication is documented empirically across four organisational roles (GenUI study, n=37, UCLA/Google), but conventions distinguishing communication artifacts from design artifacts have not been formalised in practice.

name: Prompted UI Tech Scaffolding
description: Software engineers generate low-fidelity visual scaffolding - rough screen layouts that help define UI surface before coding begins. The output is a technical reference artifact, not a design proposal: sufficient to scope implementation and support early discussion, not to represent design intent. This use case is distinct from designer-led vibe coding (CON-021): the developer drives, required fidelity is low, and the artifact serves the engineer's own planning rather than stakeholder evaluation. Director arrangement: the developer specifies what they need to advance; AI generates a working reference; the developer uses or discards without iterative refinement.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: defining when developer-generated UI remains a scoping tool and when designer involvement becomes necessary; establishing team norms that prevent implementation reference artifacts from entering design review or stakeholder channels as de facto design proposals
recognising when developer-generated UI has shaped shared expectations before design input, and intervening before those expectations constrain downstream design work

value_hypothesis.sentence: Enables software engineers to generate low-fidelity UI scaffolding for implementation scoping without designer dependency, bootstrapping the visual reference needed to start coding on small projects or low-stakes features.

value_indicator.description: elapsed time from feature requirements to implementation start relative to waiting for designer-produced spec baseline
whether developers gain a visual scoping capability that removes designer dependency for low-stakes or small-project prototyping

risk_typology.narrative: developer-generated UI enters shared channels (tickets, PRDs, Slack) and is treated as a design artifact before design has been involved; visual output signals design completion that has not occurred, generating stakeholder feedback on a scoping tool rather than a design proposal

developer-generated UI reflects implementation priorities - technical simplicity, known component patterns, build convenience - rather than user needs; when it circulates in team channels, the absence of design thinking is invisible beneath visual plausibility

developer-generated UI that is technically convenient may establish layout expectations inconsistent with design requirements discovered later, creating rework cost when actual design contradicts assumed scope

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - developer-initiated GenUI scoping is documented empirically (GenUI study, n=37, UCLA/Google), but conventions distinguishing implementation reference artifacts from design artifacts have not been formalised, and the risk of downstream misinterpretation has not been systematically addressed in practice.

name: Thematic Coding of Interview Data
description: The researcher uploads interview transcripts and works iteratively with an LLM to identify themes, cluster codes, and surface patterns. AI proposes initial codes and groupings; the researcher evaluates, reframes, merges, and challenges. The researcher holds interpretive authority - AI accelerates the mechanical work of tagging and cross- referencing while the researcher drives the analytical framework. Evidence points to a practitioner gap: those without deep qualitative research experience tend to overestimate the reliability of AI- generated codes, while experienced researchers apply appropriate caution. Practitioner background is therefore a significant variable in how safely this UC is executed.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: coding rigor, interpretive validity, and recognizing when AI-proposed codes misrepresent participant meaning
understanding what participant statements reveal about motivations, mental models, and lived experience beyond their surface content

value_hypothesis.sentence: Compresses thematic coding of qualitative interview data from days to hours while surfacing cross-participant patterns a solo researcher might miss.

value_indicator.description: elapsed time from raw transcripts to coded theme map relative to fully manual baseline
proportion of transcript data incorporated into findings relative to manual coding baseline

risk_typology.narrative: AI may generate plausible-sounding codes that do not accurately represent participant meaning; risk is amplified when the reviewer lacks qualitative expertise to detect misrepresentation
AI may flatten divergent participant perspectives into artificially coherent themes, suppressing minority voices and contradictory findings
sustained reliance on AI for coding may degrade the researcher's own ability to engage deeply with raw transcript data

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - multiple tools offer AI coding features and use is widespread informally, but integration into governed research workflows with quality checks is not yet standard.

name: Problem Statement Drafting
description: The designer or researcher provides research findings or synthesis outputs to an LLM and asks it to draft problem statements framing the design challenge. AI generates candidate statements; the practitioner evaluates for accuracy, focus, and actionability before selecting or refining. Evidence indicates AI-generated statements tend to be saturated with unrequested information - elaborating beyond what the format requires - making human editing and focusing an essential step, not an optional one.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: knowing which finding should anchor the statement, and what constitutes a well-scoped problem vs. an over-broad or assumption-laden one
ensuring the statement connects to the actual decision or design opportunity being addressed, not just a restatement of research findings

value_hypothesis.sentence: Generates candidate problem statements from synthesis inputs, reducing blank- page effort while the designer applies judgment on focus, scope, and connection to the actual design opportunity.

value_indicator.description: elapsed time from synthesis to approved problem statement
independent rating of statement actionability relative to a researcher-only drafting baseline

risk_typology.narrative: statements appear well-grounded but may embed assumptions or generalisations not directly supported by the underlying research data
AI tendency to saturate statements with unrequested information creates apparent completeness that obscures rather than clarifies the core problem
AI defaults to familiar problem statement formats, potentially constraining the range of framings the team explores

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - used informally but not yet integrated into governed synthesis or framing workflows in most organisations.

name: Persona Generation
description: The designer provides research synthesis, user data, or participant profiles and asks an LLM to generate candidate personas. AI proposes persona structures, attributes, and narratives; the designer evaluates for representativeness, relevance, and grounding in actual research. Evidence indicates output quality improves dramatically with structured prompts and meta-prompting - generic prompts yield generic personas. The human's primary role is to challenge AI-generated personas against actual research data and reject those that don't hold up.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: knowing whether a proposed persona is grounded in actual research patterns vs. stereotyped archetypes, and what participant data actually supports
evaluating whether persona motivations, behaviors, and mental models ring true against what participants actually revealed in fieldwork

value_hypothesis.sentence: Rapidly generates multiple persona candidates for designer evaluation, with output quality scaling significantly with prompt structure and specificity.

value_indicator.description: time from synthesis inputs to reviewable persona set
proportion of persona attributes traceable to actual participant data rather than AI extrapolation

risk_typology.narrative: AI personas default to demographic stereotypes and archetypal profiles, producing recognisable but hollow representations that don't reflect actual research nuance
personas appear richly detailed but attributes may be fabricated or extrapolated beyond what the underlying data supports
AI-generated personas may lack the emotional and contextual specificity that makes personas useful for generating genuine design empathy

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - persona generation with AI is becoming common but without structured prompting protocols and research- grounding checks, output quality is highly variable.

name: Journey Mapping
description: The designer provides interview transcripts, observation notes, or synthesis outputs to an AI-powered tool that generates a draft journey map - identifying stages, steps, actions, emotions, and pain points. AI structures the raw data into journey format; the designer evaluates, reorganises stages, merges steps, and applies interpretive judgment about what the journey actually reveals. Current evidence is based on a purpose-built research prototype; production-ready tools for this workflow are not yet established.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: determining the right level of granularity for stages, interpreting emotional peaks and valleys, and deciding where the journey meaningfully begins and ends
structuring the map to serve the specific design decision being made, not just to represent all data with equal weight

value_hypothesis.sentence: Transforms interview data into a structured journey map significantly faster than manual methods, enabling teams to move from raw data to shared understanding more quickly.

value_indicator.description: elapsed time from interview data to reviewable journey map draft
proportion of recurring patterns surfaced relative to a researcher-only baseline

risk_typology.narrative: standard journey map formats constrain how the experience is represented, flattening non-linear, fragmented, or context-dependent journeys into tidy sequential structures that impose order the actual experience does not have

AI-generated stages and emotional attributions may appear data-grounded but misrepresent the actual experience through two vectors: structural assumptions embedded in the tool's journey format, and limitations inherent in the source data itself - including who was interviewed, what was recorded, and what participants chose or were able to articulate
compression of rich participant experience into structured format may lose the texture and specificity that makes journey maps emotionally resonant for design teams

attestation.diagnostic: Tool gap - purpose-built AI journey mapping tools exist only as research prototypes; practitioners relying on general-purpose LLMs must construct and validate the journey structure themselves.

name: Design Brief Creation
description: The designer provides project context - client goals, research outputs, constraints, and requirements - and works iteratively with an LLM to draft a design brief. AI generates structured sections covering problem context, design goals, constraints, and success criteria; the designer evaluates, refines, and ensures the brief accurately captures the project's intent. This is one of the few UCs in the catalog supported by quantitative evidence of time reduction from a controlled study.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: translating project context, stakeholder goals, and research insights into a brief that productively constrains the design space without over-specifying
ensuring the brief accurately reflects what research revealed about users and their needs, rather than defaulting to assumed requirements

value_hypothesis.sentence: Reduces design brief creation time significantly (48.6% reduction documented) while maintaining the strategic clarity that makes briefs useful for guiding design decisions.

value_indicator.description: elapsed time from project inputs to approved design brief; 48.6% reduction documented
proportion of sections completed without major rework relative to a from-scratch baseline

risk_typology.narrative: AI defaults to familiar brief structures and phrasings, converging all briefs toward similar formulations regardless of project specifics and flattening the strategic distinctiveness that makes a brief useful
AI-generated briefs may obscure the reasoning behind design constraints, making it harder for teams to challenge or adapt the brief as the project evolves
briefs may appear strategically sound but embed assumptions about the problem space or user needs not grounded in actual research or stakeholder input

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - quantitative evidence of value exists but brief creation with AI is not yet formally integrated into design workflows or brief review governance.

name: Survey & Open-Text Analysis
description: The researcher exports open-text survey responses and runs them through an LLM or AI analytics tool to cluster responses, extract themes, and surface patterns at scale. AI performs the bulk of analytical processing autonomously; the researcher reviews output - validating theme labels, checking cluster coherence, and interpreting what patterns mean for design decisions. This UC is particularly valuable for large datasets where manual open-text coding would be prohibitively time-consuming.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: evaluating whether AI-identified themes accurately represent respondent intent, and interpreting what patterns mean for the design decision at hand
understanding clustering behaviour, sampling implications, and what constitutes meaningful vs. spurious patterns in survey data

value_hypothesis.sentence: Enables analysis of open- text survey data at a scale manual coding cannot sustain, surfacing themes and patterns across large response sets without proportional researcher time.

value_indicator.description: open-text responses processable per researcher-hour relative to manual coding baseline
proportion of meaningful response content captured in identified themes relative to a manual coding baseline

risk_typology.narrative: AI-clustered themes may appear statistically robust but misrepresent respondent intent, particularly for ambiguous or context-dependent responses
clustering tends to merge minority or outlier perspectives into dominant themes, suppressing dissenting voices that may be analytically significant
AI theme labels and cluster boundaries reflect patterns in training data and model architecture, potentially imposing external framings on what respondents actually said

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - AI text analysis tools are available and used, but integration into formal research practice with theme validation protocols is not yet standard.

name: Session Summarization & Rapid Debrief
description: After a research session, an AI tool transcribes the recording and generates a structured summary - including key quotes, themes, notable moments, and suggested highlights. The researcher reviews for accuracy and completeness before sharing with stakeholders. The primary value is speed: same-day summaries enable alignment while sessions are still fresh, without requiring the researcher to manually review and write up full session notes.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: determining which moments are analytically significant vs. tangential, and what the session means in the context of the broader study
calibrating the summary for the intended audience, adjusting tone, detail level, and emphasis for stakeholder vs. research team consumption

value_hypothesis.sentence: Enables same-day session summaries for stakeholder sharing, compressing the time between fieldwork and team alignment while reducing the manual burden of post-session writeup.

value_indicator.description: elapsed time from session end to shareable summary relative to manual writeup baseline
researcher time spent on summarisation relative to fully manual review baseline

risk_typology.narrative: AI summaries may omit significant moments, misrepresent participant intent, or foreground highlights that are vivid or quotable rather than analytically important
when AI selects highlights, the reasoning behind selection is opaque; stakeholders may accept AI-curated quotes as representative without knowing what was excluded or deprioritised
rapid summaries create an impression of synthesis completeness before genuine analysis has been done, potentially foreclosing deeper interpretation downstream

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - transcription and summarisation tools are widely adopted, but structured review protocols before stakeholder sharing are not yet standard practice.

name: Project Knowledge Base Creation
description: The designer creates a Project Knowledge Base - an ad hoc but intentionally cultivated collection of project-specific materials (design system rules, heuristics, principles, prior research, user feedback) loaded into a context the designer can query on demand. This is a project-scoped working intelligence layer, not a systematic cross-project repository: it grows through deliberate human curation rather than automated ingestion, and is distinct from the organisation-wide Research Repository covered in IMP-059. Director arrangement: the human decides what belongs, how it is structured, and what constitutes signal versus noise; AI enables retrieval and cross-referencing across the full corpus. Ongoing enrichment with validation and testing outcomes is covered in IMP-065.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: deciding what belongs in the knowledge base, how to structure it for AI consumption, and what constitutes useful vs. noisy or outdated content
curating research outputs, heuristics, and user feedback to ensure the knowledge base reflects actual evidence rather than assumptions or received wisdom

value_hypothesis.sentence: Transforms accumulated project knowledge into a queryable intelligence layer, enabling teams to surface relevant precedent, constraints, and insights on demand rather than through manual document search.

value_indicator.description: time to retrieve relevant precedent or constraint relative to manual document search baseline
proportion of accumulated project knowledge actively surfaced and applied to design decisions

risk_typology.narrative: the knowledge base creates confidence in AI-synthesized answers that may be incomplete, outdated, or misrepresent the nuance of source documents
as AI synthesizes across documents, the specific source reasoning behind an answer becomes opaque; practitioners may accept synthesized guidance without tracing it to its origin
over-reliance on the knowledge base as an oracle may reduce practitioners' direct engagement with primary sources, atrophying independent judgment

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - proven in specific organisational implementations, but constructing and maintaining a well-curated knowledge base requires sustained curation effort most teams do not yet invest.

name: Cross-Study Meta-Analysis
description: The researcher provides findings, reports, or synthesis outputs from multiple studies conducted over time, and asks an LLM to identify longitudinal patterns, recurring themes, contradictions, and emergent signals across the corpus. AI proposes cross-study connections and patterns; the researcher evaluates whether they reflect genuine longitudinal trends or are artifacts of sampling differences, study design variation, or AI pattern-matching on superficial features. The value is in surfacing meta-level insight that is difficult to achieve through manual comparison of individual study reports.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: distinguishing genuine longitudinal patterns from artifacts of study design differences, sampling variation, or AI over-generalisation across methodologically dissimilar studies
understanding what constitutes valid comparison across studies with different methodologies, participant groups, and time periods

value_hypothesis.sentence: Surfaces longitudinal patterns and cross-study signals from accumulated research that would be impractical to identify through manual comparison of individual study outputs.

value_indicator.description: number of actionable cross-study patterns identified relative to a manual meta-analysis baseline
elapsed time from research corpus to meta-analysis output relative to manual comparison baseline

risk_typology.narrative: AI may identify spurious patterns that appear meaningful but reflect methodological differences between studies rather than genuine longitudinal trends
AI pattern-matching may be skewed toward findings that resemble patterns in its training data, systematically missing novel or unexpected longitudinal signals

the basis for cross-study pattern identification is opaque; researchers may not be able to reconstruct how a pattern was derived or why certain studies were implicitly weighted more heavily than others

attestation.diagnostic: Tool gap - general-purpose LLMs can attempt cross-study synthesis, but no purpose-built workflow or tool exists for rigorous AI-assisted meta-analysis of design research corpora.

name: Layer Renaming
description: AI scans the layer tree of a design file and renames layers according to a target convention - replacing default names ("Frame 247", "Rectangle 12") with semantically meaningful labels that reflect each layer's content and role. The designer triggers the operation, reviews the results, and corrects any misattributions. Auditor arrangement: AI processes the full layer tree without step-by-step direction; the designer spot-checks output and intervenes on exceptions. The value is straightforward efficiency - manual layer renaming is tedious work that designers routinely skip under deadline pressure, resulting in illegible files at handoff. Automating the task makes clean file hygiene the default rather than the exception.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: knowing which naming conventions actually serve downstream consumers (developers, other designers navigating the file) versus merely looking tidy; structuring names to reflect the layer tree's logical hierarchy rather than just labelling individual elements
ensuring AI-applied layer names align with the team's component naming conventions so that file organisation and system architecture stay in sync

value_hypothesis.sentence: Automates the tedious manual cleanup of design file layer names, making consistent naming the default rather than a task designers skip under deadline pressure.

value_indicator.description: time previously spent on manual layer renaming per file or per sprint
proportion of layers following team naming conventions at handoff, relative to manual baseline

risk_typology.narrative: clean layer names create the appearance of a well-organised file when structural issues - wrong grouping, detached components, redundant nesting - remain unaddressed; the surface looks professional while the underlying architecture may still cause problems at handoff
AI may assign semantically incorrect names (labelling a button group as an icon, or a header as body text) that mislead developers reading the layer tree without visually inspecting each element

attestation.diagnostic: N/A

named_tools: Figma (Rename It plugin, native AI features)

name: Copy Generation and Rewriting
description: AI generates or rewrites production UI copy - button labels, error messages, empty states, tooltips, onboarding steps - based on context, target reading level, and tone guidelines. At the basic level, a designer provides screen context and AI produces candidate strings for review. The more mature practice embeds content rules into the AI's operating context: copy type classifications (informative vs. persuasive vs. transactional), tone constraints by context (error messages are direct and humourless; marketing surfaces are warmer), vocabulary boundaries, and locale-specific register shifts. Practitioner evidence describes teams building custom GPTs with brand voice guidelines, UX copywriting rules, and copy type logic, then using the AI to produce tone-and-content compliance feedback for screen-by-screen review. This points toward content operating as a structured layer within the design system rather than freeform text composed per screen. Approver arrangement: AI drafts at volume; the human reviews for brand voice, contextual fit, and interaction-level clarity before copy enters production.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: evaluating whether AI-generated copy maintains brand voice and serves the interaction context, and - in the more structured version - designing the content rules, type classifications, and tone frameworks that constrain AI generation toward consistently on-brand output
ensuring microcopy works within the flow: error messages that help recovery, labels that reduce ambiguity, onboarding text that matches the progressive disclosure model of the interface

value_hypothesis.sentence: Compresses the cycle of drafting and refining production UI copy so that screens ship with considered microcopy rather than placeholder text that never gets replaced.

value_indicator.description: elapsed time from copy request to approved production string
proportion of screens shipping with reviewed microcopy vs. placeholder or default text

risk_typology.narrative: AI-generated copy gravitates toward safe, generic phrasing that reads clearly but sounds like every other product; brand voice and distinctive tone flatten toward a median that is competent but undifferentiated

AI-generated error messages, empty states, and onboarding copy may be technically clear but miss the emotional context of the moment - frustration, confusion, first-time anxiety - that skilled microcopy acknowledges and addresses

AI may produce grammatically correct copy that is semantically wrong for the context - a confirmation message that implies an action completed when it is still processing, or a label that is accurate in isolation but ambiguous within the surrounding interface

attestation.diagnostic: N/A

name: Asset Discovery Across Files
description: AI searches across design files to locate assets that match a designer's intent - similar components, matching styles, existing patterns that could be reused or adapted rather than rebuilt. The designer describes what they need; AI returns candidates ranked by similarity. Approver arrangement: AI performs the search and surfaces candidates; the designer evaluates relevance and decides whether to reuse, adapt, or create new. The value is efficiency in large projects where the asset inventory has grown beyond what any single designer can hold in memory - especially in multi-team environments where components may exist in files the designer has never opened.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: recognising when a surfaced asset is a genuine reuse candidate versus a visual near-miss that would introduce inconsistency if adopted; understanding component architecture well enough to distinguish between assets that are structurally equivalent and those that merely look similar
evaluating whether reusing a discovered asset serves the broader organisational structure of the project or creates a misleading pattern connection

value_hypothesis.sentence: Reduces the time designers spend manually searching for existing assets across large multi-file projects, surfacing reusable components, styles, and patterns that would otherwise be recreated from scratch.

value_indicator.description: time spent locating existing assets relative to manual file-by-file search baseline
proportion of new asset creation avoided through discovery of existing reusable components

risk_typology.narrative: fast asset discovery may encourage reuse of components that are close but not appropriate, substituting speed for the design judgment needed to assess whether an existing asset genuinely fits the new context

AI-assisted discovery biases designers toward existing patterns over novel solutions; when finding an existing asset is easier than creating a new one, the path of least resistance favours visual convergence even when the design context warrants differentiation

attestation.diagnostic: N/A

name: Design-to-Development Handoff
description: AI translates completed design artifacts into developer-consumable outputs - component markup, implementation specs, annotated code snippets, or living demo pages. The enabling architecture connects AI to both the design file and the target codebase via context protocols (e.g., MCP), so the AI maps design elements to existing components and generates output that fits the team's actual code patterns rather than producing generic code from visual inspection alone. Practitioner evidence describes pipelines from design tool to codebase mapping to rules-file-governed generation, producing specification documents and living demos directly from design artifacts. The handoff gap is expensive - industry estimates put the annual cost of translation friction at six figures per product pod. Approver arrangement: AI generates handoff artifacts; the designer validates design intent and the developer validates implementation feasibility before code enters the build.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: evaluating whether AI-generated handoff artifacts accurately represent what can be built within the target platform, technology stack, and performance constraints; catching cases where AI translates the visual design faithfully but misses implementation complexity
verifying that AI-generated code correctly maps design elements to existing system components rather than generating bespoke implementations that bypass the component library

value_hypothesis.sentence: Compresses the translation cycle between finished design and developer-ready specification, reducing both elapsed handoff time and the interpretation errors that accumulate when developers reconstruct design intent from static artifacts.

value_indicator.description: elapsed time from design completion to developer-ready specification
proportion of design-intent misinterpretations caught during development relative to manual handoff baseline

risk_typology.narrative: AI-generated handoff artifacts may appear correct - syntactically valid code, plausible component mappings - while containing subtle errors: dropped UI elements, inverted interaction logic, incorrect state management, or animations that look right in spec but fail under real conditions; practitioner evidence reports developers reverting to manual builds after AI handoff output required more debugging than it saved

AI translates the visual design literally without surfacing implementation cost; complex micro-interactions, cross-platform edge cases, or performance-intensive patterns pass through the handoff unquestioned, setting developer expectations against designs that are expensive or impossible to build as specified

AI-generated code embeds implementation decisions (layout approach, state management pattern, component composition) without documenting why; developers inherit working code they cannot easily maintain or extend because the reasoning behind structural choices is absent

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - the tooling exists (context protocols, design-to-code pipelines) but reliable end-to-end workflows require significant configuration - codebase mapping, rules files, component connection - that most teams have not yet invested in.

name: Design System Documentation
description: AI generates design system documentation from existing artifacts - component specifications from design system structures, usage guidelines from component metadata, interaction documentation from prototype behaviour. The more mature practice treats component metadata as structured data (e.g., TypeScript definitions) that AI can query, auto-generate from existing documentation, and keep in sync as the system evolves. This inverts the traditional relationship between design work and documentation: instead of documentation being a separate deliverable written after the fact, it becomes a generated output that stays current because it is derived from the artifacts themselves. Approver arrangement: AI produces documentation drafts from source artifacts; the designer reviews for accuracy, completeness, and whether the text captures design intent rather than just surface properties.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: evaluating whether AI-generated documentation accurately represents component architecture, constraint relationships, and composition rules rather than just listing visible properties; ensuring documentation serves the people who need to use, extend, and maintain the system

structuring documentation for its audience: developers need implementation detail, designers need usage guidance, and product managers need rationale - AI-generated docs tend to flatten these distinctions

value_hypothesis.sentence: Reduces the effort required to produce and maintain design system documentation - component usage guidelines, interaction specifications, design rationale - so that documentation keeps pace with the design work it describes.

value_indicator.description: proportion of components with up-to-date documentation relative to manual authoring baseline
time spent writing and updating documentation per component or per release cycle

risk_typology.narrative: AI-generated documentation may describe components accurately at the surface level while missing constraint relationships, edge-case behaviour, or usage caveats that practitioners know from experience but that are not encoded in the source artifacts; the documentation looks complete but omits what matters most

when documentation is generated from artifacts rather than written by the people who made design decisions, the reasoning behind those decisions is lost; the documentation explains what the system is but not why it was designed that way
the act of writing documentation forces designers to articulate and defend their decisions; automating that step removes a cognitive forcing function that strengthens design reasoning

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - AI-generated documentation from structured metadata is demonstrated in practitioner experiments but requires investment in metadata architecture that most design systems do not yet have.

name: Design Token Management
description: AI manages design token operations - updating values, propagating changes across brands and themes, and running regression checks to surface unintended consequences before token changes reach production. The designer or design system maintainer describes the intended change (e.g., adjust the primary colour ramp, tighten spacing scale, update typographic hierarchy); AI executes the change across the token architecture, identifies downstream impacts, and flags regressions. The value scales with system complexity: a single-brand token set is manageable manually, but multi-brand, multi-platform, multi-theme architectures make manual propagation error-prone and slow. The Approver arrangement reflects the workflow: AI proposes and executes token changes; the design system maintainer reviews the change set and its downstream effects before committing.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: understanding token architecture deeply enough to evaluate whether AI-propagated changes maintain the semantic relationships between tokens (e.g., that a spacing scale adjustment preserves the intended rhythm, that colour ramp changes maintain contrast ratios across all contexts) rather than just updating values mechanically
assessing whether token changes will behave correctly across target platforms, build pipelines, and rendering contexts where token resolution may differ from what the design tool displays

value_hypothesis.sentence: Compresses the cycle of updating, propagating, and regression-testing design tokens across brands and platforms, reducing both the manual effort and the risk of inconsistency that token changes introduce at scale.

value_indicator.description: elapsed time to propagate a token change across all brands and platforms relative to manual baseline
proportion of downstream inconsistencies caught before commit relative to post-release discovery baseline

risk_typology.narrative: AI may propagate token changes that are syntactically correct but semantically wrong: updating a value without preserving the design intent behind the original token relationship, or passing regression checks that test for value consistency without testing for visual or interaction coherence

token changes that render correctly in the design tool may behave differently across platforms, browsers, or build environments; AI regression checks may validate against the design system abstraction without testing against actual implementation contexts

AI-propagated token changes create a maintenance gap: the system is updated but nobody can explain why specific values were chosen for specific contexts, making future modifications harder to reason about

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - MCP-connected token management is demonstrated by platform vendors and early-adopter teams, but the integration between AI tools, token pipelines, and build systems is not yet standardised.

name: Design Walkthroughs
description: Video demos and walkthroughs are an efficient and lightweight way of communicating design direction and work-in-progress without producing a full concept report on rationale and key choices. The designer records screen-based walkthroughs - prototype demos, flow explanations, design rationale narratives - using AI-enhanced recording tools that handle camera framing, cursor smoothing, zoom automation, and post-capture editing. AI assists with transcript generation, filler- word removal, and clip structuring, reducing the gap between raw recording and shareable artifact. Director arrangement: the designer drives all creative decisions - what to show, how to frame the narrative, what to emphasise; AI handles the production mechanics. The value is velocity: design teams that previously avoided video communication because of production overhead can now share recorded walkthroughs as easily as writing a message.
