expertise_differentiator.narrative: structuring the walkthrough narrative to communicate design intent effectively: sequencing, emphasis, pacing, and audience calibration that determine whether a recording communicates or merely documents
tailoring the walkthrough to its audience: stakeholder presentations require different framing than developer handoff recordings or team critique sessions

value_hypothesis.sentence: Compresses the production of design walkthrough videos, demo recordings, and stakeholder-facing presentations from a multi-step editing process to a near- real-time capture-and-share workflow.

value_indicator.description: elapsed time from recording to shareable artifact relative to manual screen-recording-plus- editing baseline
number of stakeholder or team touchpoints served via recorded walkthrough vs. live meeting baseline

risk_typology.narrative: the ease of producing polished video recordings may substitute for deeper engagement; stakeholders watch a walkthrough instead of interrogating the design, and the recorded format discourages the back-and-forth that live review enables

when AI handles production quality (framing, pacing, editing), designers may lose the communication craft of structuring a design narrative - a skill that transfers to live presentations, written documentation, and stakeholder conversations

attestation.diagnostic: N/A

expertise_differentiator.narrative: defining and encoding the brand rules, layout logic, and quality thresholds that govern the pipeline; the expertise is in designing the system that generates, not in generating individual assets
structuring the component library and brand assets so that AI can compose them correctly: which components combine, which layouts serve which purposes, what constraints prevent off-brand combinations

value_hypothesis.sentence: Enables non-design staff to produce on-brand collateral - presentation decks, marketing assets, internal communications - without design team involvement for every request, while maintaining brand consistency through embedded system constraints.

value_indicator.description: volume of routine collateral requests handled by the pipeline without design team involvement
proportion of pipeline-generated assets passing brand compliance review without revision

risk_typology.narrative: pipeline-generated assets will converge on the templates and patterns encoded in the system; while this ensures consistency, it also eliminates the creative variation that a designer brings to individual briefs, producing collateral that is reliably on-brand but increasingly interchangeable

AI may compose brand elements in combinations that are technically rule-compliant but visually or tonally wrong: correct logo placement with inappropriate imagery, proper typography with awkward content pacing, or colour-compliant layouts that undermine the intended hierarchy

as routine asset production moves to the pipeline, the design team loses touch with how the brand performs across everyday touchpoints; the collateral that once kept designers connected to the brand's practical application becomes invisible to them

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - the practitioner evidence describes a working pipeline but the architecture requires significant custom integration (automation platform, AI logic layer, design tool export) that is not yet replicable without dedicated engineering effort.

name: Production-Scale System-Aware Generation
description: AI generates production-quality code from design specifications, constrained by the team's design system to produce output that uses actual system components, respects token hierarchies, and follows established code patterns. Where concept-phase system- grounded drafting (CON-028) connects AI to the design system for exploratory work, this UC targets production reliability: the output must be accurate enough to enter the build pipeline without full manual reconstruction. Practitioner evidence describes techniques for achieving this: template hierarchies that provide structural scaffolding (fast mode for layout, full mode for complete implementation), JSON configuration for deterministic changes that bypass generative uncertainty, and hybrid approaches combining templates with instruction sets that reduce hallucination to near-zero for constrained operations. The principle is that AI must understand component hierarchy - not just component names - to produce output that engineering will accept. The Co-creator arrangement reflects the iterative calibration required: the designer configures constraints, evaluates output against system conformance, adjusts parameters, and re-generates until the output meets production standards.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: understanding component architecture deeply enough to configure AI constraints that produce structurally correct output: which components compose, how tokens cascade, what hierarchy rules the system enforces, and where AI reliably fails without explicit guidance
evaluating whether AI-generated code will function correctly in the target stack, build pipeline, and runtime environment, not just render correctly in preview

value_hypothesis.sentence: Generates production- ready code that conforms to the team's design system with sufficient reliability to enter the build pipeline, compressing the cycle from approved design to shippable implementation.

value_indicator.description: proportion of AI-generated code conforming to design system constraints without manual correction, relative to non-system-aware generation baseline
elapsed time from approved design to code entering the build pipeline

risk_typology.narrative: AI-generated code may use correct component names while misusing their APIs, violating composition rules, or producing layouts that render correctly in isolation but break within the broader application context; the output passes visual inspection but fails integration testing

production code generated from design specifications may not account for state management, data binding, performance constraints, or cross-platform behaviour that the design tool does not represent; code that looks production-ready in preview may require substantial rework for production deployment

as AI generates larger blocks of production code, the implementation decisions embedded in that code (layout strategy, state approach, component composition logic) become opaque to the engineers who must maintain and extend it

attestation.diagnostic: Adoption gap - the techniques for reliable production- scale generation (template hierarchies, hybrid deterministic- generative approaches) are demonstrated by innovator teams with mature design systems and dedicated engineering investment, but the configuration overhead and system maturity prerequisites limit replication.

name: Designer Code Contribution
description: The designer clones the production repository, uses an AI IDE to make design-quality changes - CSS adjustments, layout refinements, spacing corrections, responsive behaviour - and opens a pull request for engineering review. The AI IDE mediates between design intent and code: the designer describes what should change; the AI generates the implementation; the designer previews live and iterates until the result matches intent. The scope is explicitly bounded: visual polish, layout, and presentation-layer behaviour, not business logic or new feature development. Engineers review design intent directly in code rather than translating from a design artifact. Practitioner evidence describes this workflow expanding over time - starting with CSS fixes and progressing to layout changes and responsive behaviour as designer confidence and AI capability grow. The Co-creator arrangement reflects the collaboration: the designer holds design intent and evaluates visual outcomes; the AI translates intent into code changes the designer could not write independently.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: the designer's value in this workflow is knowing what the right visual and interaction outcome looks like; the AI can write code, but only the designer can evaluate whether a spacing change improves scanability, whether a responsive breakpoint preserves the intended hierarchy, or whether an animation timing feels correct

developing enough understanding of the codebase to scope changes appropriately: knowing what constitutes a contained presentation-layer change versus what touches business logic, state management, or data architecture that requires engineering ownership

value_hypothesis.sentence: Enables designers to make contained design-quality changes directly in the production codebase, bypassing the handoff cycle entirely for work that is within design's domain of expertise.

value_indicator.description: elapsed time from identifying a design-quality issue to merged code, relative to traditional handoff-and-ticket baseline
range of change types designers can ship directly, tracked over time as the workflow matures

risk_typology.narrative: the AI IDE makes code changes feel contained when they may not be; a CSS change that renders correctly in the designer's preview may break in other contexts, devices, or states that the designer does not test; the boundary between presentation-layer and structural changes is not always visible to someone without engineering training

AI-generated code changes may achieve the desired visual outcome through implementation approaches that are technically unsound: overriding styles rather than using system tokens, hardcoding values rather than using variables, or creating fixes that work locally but introduce technical debt

if designers can ship code without understanding it, the feedback loop between design decisions and implementation consequences is broken; designers may make increasingly ambitious changes without developing the technical judgment to scope them appropriately

attestation.diagnostic: Trust gap - the workflow requires engineering teams to grant designers production repository access and to trust AI-mediated code contributions; organisational willingness to extend this trust is the primary constraint, not tooling capability.

name: UI Linting and Compliance Checking
description: AI analyses UI implementations against design system rules to detect violations - incorrect token usage, component misuse, spacing deviations, hierarchy infractions - that manual code review routinely misses. The key finding from practitioner research is that neither AI alone nor deterministic heuristics alone are sufficient: AI brings flexibility to catch novel or context-dependent violations, but hallucinates false positives without structural guardrails; deterministic rules catch known violations reliably but cannot reason about intent. The emerging practice is a hybrid pipeline combining both - heuristic checks for known constraint violations, AI evaluation for contextual and intent-level compliance. Auditor arrangement: AI scans continuously or at commit time; the design system maintainer reviews flagged violations and decides which require action.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: distinguishing genuine violations from acceptable contextual overrides; understanding which rules encode hard constraints (accessibility-critical contrast ratios) versus soft guidance (preferred spacing) and calibrating the linting pipeline accordingly
designing the hybrid pipeline architecture: which checks run as deterministic rules, which require AI evaluation, and how to integrate both into the team's existing build and review process

value_hypothesis.sentence: Detects design system violations in UI implementations before they reach production, catching inconsistencies that manual review misses at scale.

value_indicator.description: proportion of design system violations caught before production relative to post-release discovery baseline
proportion of flagged violations that prove to be acceptable overrides, indicating pipeline calibration quality

risk_typology.narrative: AI-assisted linting may flag violations with high confidence that are contextually appropriate, or miss violations that require understanding design intent rather than just pattern matching; the linting report looks authoritative but its accuracy depends on how well the pipeline understands the system's actual rules versus its surface patterns

a passing lint check creates the impression of system compliance when the checks may only cover a subset of the system's constraints; teams may treat a clean lint report as proof of quality when structural, compositional, or intent-level violations go unchecked
when AI flags a violation, the reasoning may be opaque: practitioners cannot tell whether the flag reflects a genuine rule or an AI inference, making it difficult to decide whether to fix or override

attestation.diagnostic: Tool gap - hybrid AI-plus-heuristic linting pipelines are demonstrated in research but available tooling does not yet integrate both approaches in a configurable, production-ready form.

name: AI-Ready Design System Architecture
description: The design system maintainer translates existing human- readable documentation into a machine-readable architecture that AI agents can query reliably. The work has three layers: structured component metadata that encodes usage patterns, anti-patterns, selection criteria, and composition rules alongside each component; a codebase index that maps the full relationship graph - what components exist, what uses what, how deep the dependency chains run; and query protocols that teach agents how to traverse the architecture and reason over cached data rather than re-exploring on every interaction. Together these layers form a retrieval pipeline for design systems. The practical impact is significant: without this infrastructure, agents explore the codebase on each session, miss components, produce false negative recommendations, and show high variance; with it, practitioner experimentation demonstrates substantially faster and more accurate results with zero false negatives on constraint-related queries. Director arrangement: the human architects the metadata schema and evaluates whether agent queries return correct results; AI generates the structured documentation output. This is not new documentation - it is existing design knowledge reformatted for a different consumer.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: knowing what metadata AI agents actually need to use components correctly: not just names and properties but composition rules, constraint hierarchies, semantic relationships, and the selection logic that determines whether AI-generated output will be accepted or rejected by the team

structuring the metadata schema, index format, and query protocols so that AI queries resolve unambiguously; poorly structured infrastructure produces confident but wrong answers the same way a poorly structured database produces misleading query results

value_hypothesis.sentence: Structures design system knowledge into a machine-readable layer that AI agents can query, enabling downstream workflows - generation, auditing, documentation - to operate against the actual system rather than guessing from training data.

value_indicator.description: proportion of AI agent queries returning correct, complete results from the structured system versus unstructured exploration baseline
number of AI-assisted workflows (generation, auditing, documentation) that operate reliably against the structured system

risk_typology.narrative: structured metadata creates high confidence in AI agent outputs that may still be wrong: metadata that is incomplete, stale, or imprecisely authored produces results that look authoritative because they come from a structured source but miss constraints the metadata failed to capture

the metadata layer introduces an abstraction between the design system and the AI agents that consume it; when an agent produces wrong output, diagnosing whether the fault lies in the metadata, the index, the query protocol, or the agent itself requires understanding the full pipeline

investing in metadata architecture assumes the design system is stable and well- structured enough to benefit; teams with fragmented or inconsistent systems may spend significant effort structuring metadata for a system that needs architectural work first

attestation.diagnostic: Adoption gap - the methodology is documented and open-sourced by an individual practitioner, with controlled experimental evidence of effectiveness, but requires design system maturity and dedicated infrastructure investment that few teams have undertaken.

named_tools: Custom tooling (metadata schemas, codebase indexer, TOON format)

name: Token Auditing and Design System Governance
description: AI audits design token usage against encoded design intent - not just whether a token exists, but whether it is used in the correct position within the system's semantic hierarchy. Practitioner evidence describes a two-generation approach: a first-generation auditor that checks token existence (does this token resolve?), and a second-generation auditor that checks intent (is a muted foreground token used for body copy where the hierarchy calls for default? does a dropdown shadow bypass the elevation system even though its syntax is valid?). The distinction matters: syntactically correct token usage can still violate design intent, and those violations are invisible to standard linting tools. The governance model follows an audit-report-compose cycle: detect violations, surface them in structured reports, then compose fixes. The Auditor arrangement reflects autonomous operation: AI scans the system continuously or on trigger; the design system maintainer reviews flagged violations and decides which require action. This UC is distinct from DEL-054 (UI Linting) in scope: linting checks implementations against rules; governance encodes and enforces the intent behind those rules.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: encoding design intent as auditable constraints: knowing what the visual hierarchy, foreground sequence, and elevation-size coherence rules actually mean, and translating those principles into checks that AI can execute without losing the reasoning

governance decisions about what constitutes a violation versus an acceptable override require judgment about trade-offs: strictness that prevents drift versus flexibility that allows contextual adaptation

value_hypothesis.sentence: Detects design token violations that encode design intent, not just syntactic correctness - catching misuse that passes standard linting but undermines the system's semantic coherence.

value_indicator.description: proportion of semantically incorrect token usage caught, relative to syntax-only checking baseline
effort required to maintain system coherence per release cycle, relative to manual audit baseline

risk_typology.narrative: AI may flag violations based on pattern matching rather than genuine understanding of design intent; the audit report looks rigorous but may miss subtle violations while flagging acceptable contextual overrides, creating false confidence in system health

when AI flags a token usage as an intent violation, the reasoning behind the flag may be opaque; maintainers cannot tell whether the AI is enforcing an encoded rule or inferring one, making triage difficult

overly strict automated governance may eliminate the contextual variation that keeps a design system flexible; if every deviation is flagged, practitioners default to the safest choice rather than the best choice

attestation.diagnostic: Adoption gap - intent-level token auditing is demonstrated by an individual practitioner with open-sourced tooling, but encoding design intent as executable constraints requires deep system knowledge and architectural maturity that few teams possess.

name: Codebase-Extracted Design System
description: AI analyses an existing production codebase and extracts a design system from it: component inventory, markup documentation, living demo pages, and rules covering mobile behaviour, accessibility, content patterns, visual language, and interaction conventions. The direction is the reverse of most design system work - instead of building a system and then implementing it in code, this UC starts with working code and derives the system from what already exists. The value is strongest for teams that have a functioning product with implicit design patterns but no formalised design system: the AI surfaces the conventions that are already embedded in the codebase and structures them into an explicit, maintainable system. The Approver arrangement reflects the workflow: AI generates the system extraction; the designer reviews whether the AI-identified patterns genuinely represent intentional design decisions versus incidental implementation choices that should not be codified.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: distinguishing between patterns that represent genuine design decisions worth codifying and patterns that are implementation accidents, legacy choices, or inconsistencies that should be resolved rather than formalised
organising the extracted components, tokens, and conventions into a coherent system structure that supports ongoing use and maintenance, not just documenting what currently exists

value_hypothesis.sentence: Generates a structured design system - markup documentation, living demos, component inventory - from an existing production codebase, creating a system where none formally existed.

value_indicator.description: elapsed time from codebase analysis to usable design system documentation relative to manual audit-and-build baseline
proportion of production UI patterns captured in the extracted system

risk_typology.narrative: AI may identify and codify patterns that are incidental rather than intentional: inconsistent spacing values formalised as a scale, accessibility violations documented as conventions, or legacy compromises elevated to design decisions; the extracted system inherits the codebase's flaws as if they were features
extracting a system from existing code locks in the current patterns; the resulting system may constrain future design work to existing conventions rather than enabling intentional evolution
AI-extracted patterns have no documented reasoning behind them; the system describes what exists but not why, making it difficult to know which conventions are load-bearing and which can be changed

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - the capability is demonstrated in a single practitioner workflow but the extraction process requires significant human judgment to separate intentional patterns from implementation artifacts, and no standardised tooling exists for the full pipeline.

name: Visual Regression Testing
description: AI compares rendered UI against baselines - either previous builds or design specifications - to detect unintended visual changes: layout shifts, colour deviations, spacing regressions, missing elements, or rendering differences across browsers and viewports. The tooling integrates into both component development environments and CI/CD pipelines, providing inline visual tests during development and dashboard-level traceability (including commit SHA linkage) for release governance. Design-to-code comparison extends the capability beyond code-to-code regression: the design file becomes the baseline, and AI flags where the implementation diverges from the intended design. The Auditor arrangement reflects autonomous operation: AI runs visual comparisons on every commit or build; the designer or developer reviews flagged differences and decides whether each represents a regression, an intentional change, or a false positive.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: evaluating whether a flagged visual difference represents a genuine regression that affects user experience or a tolerable rendering variation; not every pixel difference is a problem, and the expertise is in knowing which differences matter

calibrating comparison thresholds and baseline management so that the testing pipeline catches meaningful deviations without generating excessive false positives from acceptable component-level variation

value_hypothesis.sentence: Detects unintended visual changes between design specifications and code implementations automatically, catching regressions that manual review misses at scale.

value_indicator.description: proportion of visual regressions reaching production relative to pre- automation baseline
ratio of genuine regressions to total flagged differences, indicating pipeline calibration quality

risk_typology.narrative: a passing visual regression suite creates confidence that the UI is correct when the tests may only cover a subset of states, viewports, or interaction conditions; teams may treat a clean report as comprehensive quality assurance when significant visual issues exist in untested paths

AI-assisted comparison may approve implementations that are pixel-accurate but behaviourally wrong: a button that renders correctly but has lost its hover state, a layout that matches the baseline at one viewport but breaks at another that is not in the test suite
automated visual testing may reduce the practice of manual visual review that builds the designer's eye for subtle rendering issues, interaction-state coverage, and cross-browser awareness

attestation.diagnostic: N/A

name: Functional Specification from Prototype
description: An AI agent analyses working prototype code and generates the functional specification that would traditionally be written before development: user stories, interaction flows, edge cases, state management rules, and data requirements. The product owner reviews and validates the specification rather than writing it from scratch. The agent is preconditioned to explain non-technically and to challenge gaps - identifying missing boundary conditions, undefined limits, or unhandled states that the prototype implements implicitly but the specification must make explicit. The specification stays accurate because it is re-generated from the code on each iteration rather than maintained as a separate document that drifts. Practitioner evidence describes the full chain - working prototype to functional spec to process diagrams - completing in minutes what traditionally takes days or weeks of manual writing. This UC is distinct from DEL-047 (Design System Documentation): that UC documents the system's components and their usage; this UC documents a product's intended behaviour and requirements.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: evaluating whether the AI-generated specification accurately captures the product intent behind the prototype's behaviour, not just describing what the code does but articulating why it does it and what business value each interaction serves

reviewing whether the specification correctly identifies edge cases, state transitions, and error conditions that the prototype handles implicitly but that must be explicitly documented for development and QA

value_hypothesis.sentence: Generates accurate functional specifications from working prototype code, inverting the traditional write-then-build sequence so that documentation is derived from working software rather than preceding it.

value_indicator.description: elapsed time from working prototype to reviewed functional specification relative to manual authoring baseline
proportion of documented requirements that accurately reflect prototype behaviour, measured against implementation review

risk_typology.narrative: AI may generate specifications that accurately describe the prototype's current behaviour while missing the intended behaviour: bugs documented as features, placeholder logic described as final implementation, or edge cases the prototype doesn't handle presented as deliberately out of scope

specifications generated from code describe what the system does but not the reasoning behind design decisions; when requirements are later questioned, the specification cannot explain why a particular interaction pattern was chosen

the act of writing functional specifications forces product owners and designers to think through requirements systematically; automating that process removes a cognitive discipline that catches gaps before they become implementation problems

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - the reverse-specification workflow is demonstrated in a single practitioner context with strong results, but requires a prototype-first development culture that inverts most organisations' existing process.

name: Bi-Directional Design-Code Sync
description: AI mediates a bi-directional workflow between design tool and codebase: the designer drafts in the design tool, AI generates a code implementation, changes push back to the design tool for visual comparison and annotation, modifications in either environment propagate to the other. This enables workflows that were previously impractical - generating multiple layout variants side-by-side in the design tool while keeping code as the functional source of truth, or routing a code prototype through the design tool for stakeholder annotation before returning to code. The capability depends on recent advances in design tool APIs that opened write access (previously read-only), making true round-trip synchronisation technically possible for the first time. The Co-creator arrangement reflects the alternating initiative: the designer works in the design tool, AI translates to code; the developer or designer modifies in code, AI reflects changes back to design. Neither environment is authoritative - the workflow treats both as valid working surfaces for different tasks.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: maintaining design coherence across round-trip translations: each pass through AI introduces potential drift, and the designer must evaluate whether the synchronised output preserves the intended interaction quality or has degraded through successive translations
understanding the limits of bi-directional sync: which properties translate cleanly between design tool and code, which lose fidelity, and where manual intervention is needed to maintain parity

value_hypothesis.sentence: Enables design and code to stay synchronised through round-trip updates: draft in the design tool, generate in code, push back for visual comparison, modify in either environment, and keep both current.

value_indicator.description: proportion of design properties that survive a round-trip translation between design tool and code without manual correction
elapsed time for a design-code-design round trip relative to manual re-implementation baseline

risk_typology.narrative: each translation pass between design tool and code introduces potential information loss; properties that don't map cleanly between environments may be silently dropped, approximated, or misinterpreted, creating subtle divergence that compounds over multiple round trips

bi-directional sync may create the expectation that design tool and code are interchangeable working surfaces when significant categories of design and code properties cannot be reliably synchronised; teams may plan workflows around a level of sync fidelity that the tooling cannot sustain
when changes flow automatically between environments, tracing which modification originated where and why becomes difficult; the design-code relationship loses its audit trail

attestation.diagnostic: Tool gap - bi-directional sync depends on design tool API capabilities (specifically write access) that have only recently become available; the workflow is technically possible but tooling maturity and reliability are unproven at scale.

name: Domain Literature Review
description: The designer or researcher prompts an LLM with a domain, product space, or research question and uses iterative dialogue to rapidly surface relevant literature, precedent, and domain knowledge. AI draws on its training data to provide an initial landscape map; the practitioner evaluates relevance, identifies gaps, and directs deeper exploration. The intended outcome is faster domain fluency at project start - reducing the time needed to achieve sufficient context to design meaningful research questions or culturally grounded solutions.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: knowing which sources matter, how to evaluate credibility, and what gaps in the landscape signal
connecting domain knowledge to the specific problem space and stakeholder context
understanding market behavior, user motivations, and cultural patterns embedded in the literature

value_hypothesis.sentence: Compresses the time required to achieve domain fluency at project start, enabling practitioners to engage meaningfully with experts and design contextually informed research plans sooner.

value_indicator.description: elapsed time from project start to sufficient domain fluency to draft a research plan
breadth of relevant domain concepts surfaced relative to a manual literature review baseline

risk_typology.narrative: AI may fabricate citations, misattribute claims, or present outdated information as current
training data skews toward mainstream English-language sources, potentially missing regional, niche, or contradictory literature
AI summarization may flatten divergent perspectives and suppress minority positions in the literature

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - practitioners at multiple organizations are using general-purpose LLMs for this informally, but structured workflows are not yet documented or standardized in design practice.

name: Research Plan Drafting
description: The researcher feeds a PRD, brief, or problem statement - including available budget, team size, and timeline constraints - into an LLM and receives a draft research plan covering research questions, methodology, participant criteria, and schedule. AI produces a structurally complete starting point scoped to stated constraints; the researcher then interrogates it for missing hypotheses, methodological fit, and relevance to the specific problem at hand. Evidence suggests AI output is plausible and well-formed but tends toward the generic - hypothesis rigor and study-specific judgment must come from the researcher.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: hypothesis formation, methodology selection, study design, and bias anticipation
aligning research questions to the actual decision the plan is meant to inform

value_hypothesis.sentence: Reduces blank-page effort for research plan creation by generating an ~80% draft from project inputs, freeing the researcher to focus on hypothesis formation and methodological judgment.

value_indicator.description: elapsed time from brief/PRD to approved research plan draft
proportion of plan sections usable without major rework relative to a from-scratch baseline

risk_typology.narrative: plans appear methodologically sound but are generic; AI defaults to familiar approaches without genuinely engaging the specific research problem or its context
AI systematically favours canonical methods, crowding out unconventional but more appropriate approaches for a given problem
repeated reliance on AI drafts may atrophy the researcher's ability to construct plans from first principles

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - used informally by multiple practitioners but not yet integrated into formal research operations or reviewed as part of standard research governance.

name: Interview Question Development
description: The researcher provides research goals and a participant profile, then uses an LLM to brainstorm a broad set of candidate interview questions from multiple angles - including perspectives the researcher may not have considered. AI generates the raw material; the researcher evaluates each question for bias, clarity, and appropriateness, then selects and sequences the final set into an interview guide. Evidence suggests AI-generated questions can confuse interviewees when not evaluated for cognitive load and context-appropriateness before use.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: recognizing leading or loaded phrasing, sequencing logic, bias avoidance, and knowing which questions will yield useful data vs. noise
anticipating how specific participant populations will process and respond to questions, including cognitive load and emotional register

value_hypothesis.sentence: Widens the question bank by generating candidates from multiple perspectives the researcher may not have considered, reducing blind spots before guide construction begins.

value_indicator.description: number of distinct question angles generated relative to a researcher-only brainstorm baseline
elapsed time from research brief to approved question set ready for guide construction

risk_typology.narrative: questions appear well-formed but may be confusing, leading, or poorly calibrated to the participant group; evidence: some AI-generated questions confused interviewees
AI lacks understanding of how a specific participant population will experience questions emotionally and cognitively
AI defaults to conventional question formats, potentially limiting the creative and contextual range of inquiry

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - LLMs are used informally for question brainstorming but the practice is undocumented and unevaluated in most research teams.

name: Research Critique & Bias Audit
description: The researcher shares a draft research plan with an LLM and asks it to take an adversarial stance - critiquing assumptions, questioning methodology choices, identifying blind spots, and surfacing potential biases in the research design. Unlike co-creation, the AI here is explicitly a challenger: it proposes critiques that the researcher then evaluates, accepts, or rebuts. Recommended practice extends this into a formal bias audit after synthesis, prompting AI to identify which participant segments or perspectives may have been underweighted in the analysis. Note: the post-synthesis bias audit dimension of this UC overlaps with the Validate phase; it is catalogued here because the primary evidence positions it as a design-time practice.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: evaluating which critiques are valid vs. spurious, and knowing when to hold the original design against AI challenge
bias detection, fairness evaluation, and recognizing whose perspectives are structurally absent from the research design

value_hypothesis.sentence: Reduces the risk of flawed research design by subjecting plans to structured adversarial critique, surfacing blind spots and biases before they corrupt data collection or synthesis.

value_indicator.description: number of actionable critiques identified relative to an unreviewed plan baseline
proportion of potential bias types surfaced relative to a structured bias checklist

risk_typology.narrative: AI critique appears comprehensive but misses domain-specific, cultural, or contextually specific blind spots that require human expertise to detect
the bias-checking tool itself carries embedded biases, shaping which blind spots get surfaced and which are systematically missed
researcher treats AI sparring as validation rather than genuine critical interrogation, accepting the process as sufficient without scrutinizing the substance

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - used informally across multiple organizations but no structured protocol exists for AI-facilitated bias auditing in design research practice.

name: Participant Recruitment Outreach
description: The researcher provides participant data - demographics, screener responses, recruitment source - and asks an LLM to draft personalized screening or outreach emails tailored to each candidate. AI generates the communications; the researcher reviews for accuracy, tone, and appropriateness before sending. The primary value is personalization at scale: reaching many candidates with individually adapted messages without proportional time investment. This UC is scoped to in-house recruitment where the researcher controls outreach directly. In agency-mediated recruitment the researcher is not the sender, but AI use by the agency on the researcher's behalf may carry consent and compliance implications that warrant explicit governance - particularly where participants may not know AI was involved in their recruitment.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: understanding participant profile requirements, screening criteria, and what constitutes a valid recruit for the specific study
tone, clarity, and persuasiveness of outreach communications calibrated to the participant population

value_hypothesis.sentence: Enables personalized participant outreach at scale, improving response rates while reducing the manual effort of tailoring individual recruitment communications.

value_indicator.description: participant response rate relative to generic outreach baseline
researcher time spent per outreach message relative to manual drafting baseline

risk_typology.narrative: personalized emails may contain inaccuracies about the participant or study that damage trust or disqualify a candidate before contact is made
AI may not calibrate tone appropriately for sensitive or vulnerable participant populations
messaging patterns may subtly favour certain participant types, skewing who responds and ultimately who gets recruited into the study

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - AI email drafting is widely adopted generally, but its application to research participant recruitment with per-candidate personalization is not yet formally documented or integrated into recruitment workflows.

name: Competitive Landscape Analysis
description: The researcher or designer prompts an LLM with a product category, market space, or design problem to generate an initial map of competing products, services, or approaches. AI draws on training data to surface known players, patterns, and precedents; the practitioner evaluates coverage, identifies gaps, and directs deeper analysis of specific competitors. Evidence indicates AI may simultaneously miss established competitors while surfacing obscure ones - making human judgment on coverage essential before the landscape is used to inform decisions.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: knowing which competitive set is relevant, evaluating market coverage, and identifying what is absent
framing the competitive landscape in terms of the specific strategic or design decision being made

value_hypothesis.sentence: Generates a rapid first-pass competitive map, reducing time to initial landscape understanding while surfacing adjacent players and unexpected entrants that structured desk research might miss.

value_indicator.description: elapsed time from brief to initial competitive map ready for validation
number of relevant competitors surfaced relative to a manual desk research baseline

risk_typology.narrative: the landscape appears complete but has systematic gaps; established players may be absent while obscure ones are included, creating a distorted picture of the market

training data skews toward English- language and digitally prominent companies, and reflects the market only up to the model's knowledge cutoff date; regional players, offline businesses, and competitors that emerged after that date are structurally invisible to the model
team accepts AI-generated landscape as authoritative without validation, closing the research loop before significant players have been accounted for

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - LLMs are used informally for competitive scanning but no structured workflow with coverage validation protocols exists in most design or research teams.

name: User Interviews (AI-Moderated)
description: The researcher defines research goals and session parameters; an AI moderator conducts asynchronous interviews with participants at scale, following a guide it generates from the researcher's brief. The AI handles session execution, transcription, tagging, and thematic analysis, returning structured reports to the researcher for review. This enables qualitative interview research at a scale and speed not possible with human moderation - running sessions simultaneously across many participants without researcher presence during fieldwork. The researcher's involvement is front-loaded (goal-setting, guide approval) and back-loaded (report review and interpretation). During sessions the human is absent, placing execution closer to Auditor territory even as the overall arrangement is Approver for output gating. This UC is catalogued in Explore as a primary discovery method. It extends into Validate when used for concept or prototype evaluation.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: defining research goals with sufficient precision to constrain AI moderation effectively, and interpreting outputs with appropriate skepticism
anticipating where AI moderation will fail to probe adequately or adapt to unexpected participant responses

value_hypothesis.sentence: Enables qualitative interview research at participant scales not feasible with human moderation, compressing fieldwork timelines while generating structured, traceable outputs ready for analysis.

value_indicator.description: interviews completable per researcher-hour relative to human-moderated baseline
time from session close to analysis-ready structured report

risk_typology.narrative: AI moderation may appear thorough but fail in ways that are hard to detect: inadequate probing, missed follow-up opportunities, surface-level responses accepted without challenge, and outputs that look analytically rigorous but misrepresent participant intent

AI moderator cannot read participant affect, adapt to emotional cues, or respond to distress the way a skilled human moderator can; sensitive topics and vulnerable populations are particularly exposed
uniform AI moderation style across participants may suppress the probe variation that surfaces divergent or unexpected findings

attestation.diagnostic: Trust gap - purpose-built AI moderation tools exist and workflows are documented, but researchers remain understandably reluctant to delegate session conduct entirely to AI, particularly for exploratory or high-stakes studies.

name: Diary Study Prompting (AI-Adaptive)
description: Diary studies involve participants logging experiences, behaviors, or feelings over time in response to researcher prompts. In this enhanced variant, an LLM analyzes each participant's prior entries and proposes adapted prompts tailored to their emerging narrative - surfacing threads worth pursuing, noting gaps, and adjusting prompt cadence based on engagement patterns. The researcher reviews proposed adaptations before deployment, maintaining methodological control while allowing the study to respond dynamically to what participants are actually reporting.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: knowing which emerging threads are methodologically significant vs. participant idiosyncrasies, and when adaptation serves the research vs. introduces confound
understanding participant engagement patterns over time, and when adaptive prompting may feel intrusive, leading, or overwhelming

value_hypothesis.sentence: Generates richer longitudinal data by dynamically adapting prompts to each participant's emerging narrative, surfacing threads a fixed prompt schedule would miss.

value_indicator.description: depth and specificity of diary entries relative to a static-prompt study baseline
proportion of participant- specific threads pursued relative to a fixed prompt schedule

risk_typology.narrative: AI adaptations inject researcher-like decisions throughout the study at scale; those decisions carry the AI's biases and are harder to audit retrospectively than a static prompt set
adapted prompts may appear contextually sensitive but pursue superficial narrative threads while missing deeper or more structurally significant patterns
AI may not recognise when entries signal participant distress, fatigue, or disengagement, continuing to prompt when a human researcher would pause or check in

attestation.diagnostic: Tool gap - the concept is documented but no off-the-shelf tool supports AI-adaptive diary prompting; practitioners must build custom integrations between an LLM and a diary study platform.

name: Response Quality Screening
description: After data collection, participant responses are run through an AI quality detection system that flags low-quality submissions - identifying patterns consistent with bot activity, professional survey takers, inattentive respondents, or internally contradictory answers. The AI operates across the full response set autonomously; the researcher reviews flagged cases and makes final inclusion or exclusion decisions. This is particularly valuable at scale, where manual review of every response is impractical and quality failures would corrupt downstream synthesis. Note: this UC addresses data quality at the point of collection. It is distinct from EXP-005, which addresses participant recruitment and outreach before fieldwork begins.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: defining quality thresholds appropriate to the specific study, understanding what constitutes acceptable variation vs. disqualifying noise, and making final inclusion and exclusion calls
ensuring exclusion decisions are fair, consistent, and do not systematically remove valid responses from underrepresented or atypical participant groups

value_hypothesis.sentence: Automates response quality screening at scale, removing corrupted or low-quality data before it influences synthesis, without requiring manual review of every submission.

value_indicator.description: proportion of low-quality responses correctly flagged relative to a manual expert review baseline
researcher time spent on quality review per 100 responses relative to a fully manual baseline

risk_typology.narrative: quality detection models define normative response patterns based on majority-population behaviour, and may systematically flag valid responses from non-native speakers, older adults, people with disabilities, or participants from different cultural contexts as fraudulent or low-quality
AI flagging appears reliable but misclassification rates may be significant and unevenly distributed across participant groups, distorting the final sample in ways that are not visible in the output
AI exclusion decisions may lack auditable reasoning, making it difficult to justify participant removal in research governance or ethics review contexts

attestation.diagnostic: Adoption gap - quality detection tools and methods are established in survey research broadly, but AI-assisted screening is variably adopted in design research; many teams still rely on manual checks or simple attention traps.

name: Research Repository Push
description: AI turns accumulated research - interview transcripts, usability reports, survey results, prior synthesis documents - into a searchable, queryable knowledge system that actively delivers relevant findings when new questions arise. The shift is from pull to push: instead of researchers manually searching archives to check whether a question has been studied before, the system identifies relevant prior evidence and delivers it at the point of need. Challenger arrangement: AI proposes connections between current questions and past findings; the researcher evaluates whether those connections are genuinely relevant or superficially similar. This UC is distinct from DEF-017 (Project Knowledge Base Creation), which covers building the knowledge base as a structured asset; this UC covers the ongoing activation of that asset - making stored knowledge discoverable and actionable across projects and over time.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: evaluating whether AI-surfaced prior findings are genuinely applicable to the current question or merely topically adjacent; the expertise is in knowing when prior evidence transfers and when context has changed enough to invalidate it
structuring the repository so that AI retrieval finds the right level of abstraction: raw transcripts for reanalysis, synthesised findings for quick reference, methodological notes for replication

value_hypothesis.sentence: Transforms static research archives into queryable knowledge systems that surface relevant prior findings at the point of decision, reducing redundant research and connecting past insights to current questions.

value_indicator.description: proportion of new research questions that are partially or fully addressable from existing repository evidence
elapsed time from question formulation to retrieval of relevant prior findings, relative to manual archive search baseline

risk_typology.narrative: AI may surface prior findings that appear relevant but are drawn from studies with different user populations, product contexts, or methodological assumptions; the connection looks valid but transfers conclusions that do not apply

the existence of a queryable repository creates confidence that prior research is being leveraged when the retrieval may be shallow, returning keyword matches rather than substantively relevant findings
researchers who rely on AI-surfaced prior work may lose the practice of deep archive exploration that occasionally finds unexpected connections a keyword-based system would miss

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - the underlying capability (semantic search over research archives) exists, but integration into ongoing research workflows as a proactive push system rather than a passive search tool is undeveloped.

name: A/B Automatization
description: AI generates A/B test designs - hypotheses, variant configurations, audience segmentation, success metrics, and sample size calculations - from product analytics and behavioural data, then recommends optimizations based on test outcomes. The integrated pipeline connects analytics to experimentation to feature management: AI analyses usage patterns to propose what to test, configures the experiment, monitors results, and suggests next actions including feature flag changes. Approver arrangement: AI proposes complete test designs and interprets results; the product designer or PM reviews whether hypotheses are strategically sound and recommendations align with product direction. The value is strongest when experimentation volume outpaces the team's capacity to manually design and interpret tests - AI handles the mechanical configuration work so practitioners can focus on choosing what questions matter. Platform note: A/B testing on native mobile is significantly harder than on web. Variant delivery requires SDK integration (Firebase A/B Testing, Amplitude Experiment), app store review cycles constrain rollback speed, and touch-based behavioural metrics are noisier than web click data. This UC applies most cleanly to web and hybrid products; mobile teams should validate toolchain compatibility before committing to an automated experimentation pipeline.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: evaluating whether AI-generated test designs are statistically sound: correct sample sizes, appropriate success metrics, valid segmentation, and results interpretation that accounts for confounding variables
determining which hypotheses are worth testing given product strategy, and whether AI-recommended optimizations serve short-term metrics at the expense of longer-term product coherence

value_hypothesis.sentence: Compresses the cycle from hypothesis to experiment to result by generating test configurations, audience segments, and success metrics from product analytics, then recommending optimizations based on outcomes.

value_indicator.description: elapsed time from hypothesis to statistically significant result, relative to manually designed experiments
proportion of experiments that produce actionable results versus inconclusive or methodologically flawed outcomes

risk_typology.narrative: high experiment volume creates the appearance of data-driven optimization when many tests may be trivial, poorly designed, or testing variations that do not meaningfully affect user outcomes; velocity is not the same as learning

AI-generated hypotheses and segmentation may embed assumptions from historical data, systematically testing variations that optimise for existing user patterns while missing opportunities to serve underrepresented segments
automating test design may reduce practitioners' fluency with experimental methodology, making them dependent on AI-generated configurations they cannot critically evaluate

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - toolchain integration between analytics, experimentation, and feature management exists in specialist platforms but automated end-to-end pipelines are not yet standard workflow in most design and product teams.

name: DS Compliance Monitoring
description: AI continuously monitors production implementations against design system specifications - component usage, token application, spacing rules, interaction patterns - detecting violations that accumulate as products evolve across teams and release cycles. The distinction from Deliver-phase UCs is operational scope: DEL-054 (UI Linting) and DEL-056 (Token Auditing) build the detection infrastructure and define the rules; this UC runs that infrastructure continuously against live, evolving products and acts on the results over time. The monitoring cycle is ongoing: AI scans implementations on a schedule or trigger, generates structured compliance reports, and tracks violation trends across releases. Practitioner evidence reports measurable outcomes - fewer inconsistencies and reduced design debt - when automated monitoring replaces periodic manual review. Auditor arrangement: AI runs compliance scans without human initiation; the design system maintainer reviews reports, prioritises violations, and decides which require remediation versus acceptable contextual overrides.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: determining which violations represent genuine drift that undermines system coherence versus contextual overrides that serve legitimate product needs; not every deviation is a problem, and enforcement without judgment creates rigidity
prioritising which compliance issues to address given team capacity and release schedules, and communicating findings in terms that product stakeholders can act on

value_hypothesis.sentence: Continuously monitors live product implementations against design system rules, detecting drift, inconsistencies, and shadow implementations before they compound into systemic design debt.

value_indicator.description: proportion of design system violations detected and resolved per release cycle, relative to manual audit baseline
measured decrease in inconsistencies and shadow implementations over time

risk_typology.narrative: a clean compliance report creates confidence that the design system is healthy when the monitoring may only cover a subset of rules, components, or product surfaces; teams may treat automated compliance as comprehensive quality assurance when significant drift exists in unscanned areas

overly strict automated enforcement may eliminate the contextual variation that keeps products usable; if every deviation is flagged, teams default to literal compliance rather than appropriate adaptation

when AI flags a violation, the reasoning may be opaque: is it enforcing an explicit rule or inferring one from patterns? Design system maintainers cannot triage effectively without understanding the basis for each flag

attestation.diagnostic: N/A

name: Queryable Personas
description: AI embodies research-grounded personas as conversational agents - custom GPTs or equivalent - that stakeholders can query to explore user perspectives without waiting for a researcher to interpret findings. A PM considering a feature trade-off can ask the persona how it would respond; a developer evaluating an error message can test wording against the persona's comprehension level. The researcher builds the persona from actual research data (interview transcripts, survey results, behavioural observations), not from demographic assumptions, and defines the boundaries of what the persona can and cannot reliably represent. The Challenger arrangement reflects the intended use: the persona challenges stakeholder assumptions by grounding responses in research evidence; stakeholders decide how to act on the challenge. This UC is distinct from DEF-012 (Persona Generation), which creates the persona artifact itself; this UC makes that artifact interactive and deployable as a decision-support tool across the organisation.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: grounding the interactive persona in actual research data rather than stereotypical assumptions, and defining the boundaries of the persona's representational validity: what questions it can answer reliably versus where it would be fabricating
calibrating the persona's responses to reflect genuine user psychology, motivation patterns, and decision-making rather than rational-actor simplifications that AI defaults toward

value_hypothesis.sentence: Transforms static persona documents into conversational agents that stakeholders can query directly, making research findings accessible at the point of product decisions rather than confined to reports.

value_indicator.description: frequency and depth of stakeholder interactions with research personas, relative to traditional persona document consultation
proportion of product decisions where stakeholders consulted research evidence (via persona or otherwise) versus relying on assumption

risk_typology.narrative: the conversational persona may generate plausible-sounding responses that go beyond the underlying research data, fabricating user perspectives that feel authentic but are not grounded in evidence; stakeholders cannot distinguish data-backed responses from AI extrapolation

reducing a user segment to a chatbot interaction may strip away the emotional and contextual complexity that makes personas useful; the convenience of querying a bot may replace the deeper understanding that comes from reading actual user quotes and stories
the persona inherits both the biases in the original research (who was interviewed, what was asked) and the biases in the AI model, compounding representational distortion

attestation.diagnostic: Trust gap - practitioners express concern about stakeholders treating AI personas as substitutes for actual user contact, and about the credibility risk when a persona generates responses not supported by the underlying research.

name: VOC and Social Listening
description: AI agents monitor voice-of-customer channels - support tickets, app reviews, social mentions, in-product feedback, NPS comments - and detect meaning-based patterns that signal emerging user needs, sentiment shifts, or experience breakdowns. Unlike keyword monitoring, LLM-powered listening detects patterns across varied vocabulary: users describing the same loading problem in ten different ways will surface as a single coherent signal. When patterns cross defined thresholds the agent can auto-deploy targeted micro-surveys to validate signals before escalating. The shift is from episodic research (periodic studies that capture a snapshot) to continuous monitoring (a persistent listening layer that flags changes in real time). Auditor arrangement: AI monitors and flags autonomously; the researcher evaluates whether flagged signals represent genuine shifts or noise and decides whether to investigate further.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: distinguishing genuine signals from noise in AI-flagged patterns: a spike in negative sentiment may reflect a real experience problem, a seasonal pattern, or a single viral complaint amplified beyond its significance
calibrating monitoring thresholds and trigger conditions so that the system flags meaningful shifts without overwhelming the team with false positives or missing slow-moving trends

value_hypothesis.sentence: Maintains a persistent listening layer across voice-of-customer channels, detecting emerging user needs, sentiment shifts, and experience breakdowns without requiring dedicated research cycles.

value_indicator.description: how far in advance of traditional research cycles the monitoring system detects emerging issues
proportion of AI-flagged patterns that, on researcher review, represent actionable findings versus noise

risk_typology.narrative: AI may detect patterns in voice-of-customer data that are statistically present but substantively meaningless: correlations in language that reflect phrasing trends rather than experience shifts, or sentiment analysis that misreads sarcasm, cultural expression, or domain-specific language

a functioning monitoring dashboard creates the appearance of continuous discovery when the team may not have the capacity to act on flagged signals; monitoring without response is surveillance, not research

monitoring channels systematically overrepresent users who leave feedback (typically the most frustrated or most enthusiastic) and underrepresent the silent majority; AI analysis inherits and may amplify this selection bias

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - keyword-based VoC monitoring is established; LLM-powered semantic monitoring with threshold- triggered validation is documented in specialist platforms but not yet standard operational practice in most design or product teams.

name: Project Knowledge Base Enrichment
description: The designer enriches the project knowledge base - the ad hoc but cultivated collection of project materials, design system rules, principles, and heuristics created in DEF-017 and governed by the instruction files from CON-024 - with outcomes from validation, testing, and live product performance. Usability test findings, A/B test results, accessibility audit outcomes, and usage analytics feed back into the knowledge base so that future AI-assisted work starts from updated evidence rather than stale assumptions. The project knowledge base is not a full research repository (see IMP-059); it is a working collection that accumulates project- specific learnings and quality controls over time. Director arrangement: the designer decides what evidence to incorporate and which prior assumptions the new evidence invalidates; AI assists with formatting and integration but cannot judge what warrants updating.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: evaluating which outcomes are robust enough to update the project knowledge base versus which are preliminary, context-specific, or methodologically weak; not every test result should become a permanent reference
structuring feedback so that new evidence connects to and updates existing knowledge rather than accumulating as an unsorted append-only log

value_hypothesis.sentence: Closes the loop between design outcomes and the project knowledge base by systematically feeding testing results, usage data, and validation findings back into the context that governs future AI-assisted work.

value_indicator.description: proportion of knowledge base entries that reflect evidence from the most recent product cycle versus entries based on outdated or superseded findings
elapsed time from outcome (test result, usage data) to knowledge base update, relative to manual curation baseline

risk_typology.narrative: maintaining a knowledge base creates the appearance of organisational learning when the feedback loop may be superficial: results logged but not synthesised, entries updated but not connected to the decisions they should inform

AI-assisted integration may introduce errors when formatting or restructuring findings for the knowledge base, subtly distorting results in ways that compound over time as future work builds on the distorted evidence
automating the feedback loop may reduce the reflective practice of manually reviewing outcomes and updating mental models - the cognitive work of synthesis that builds expertise

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - individual practitioners maintain project knowledge bases and feed outcomes back into them, but the systematic enrichment loop - from validation outcomes to knowledge base to improved future generation - is not yet an established organisational workflow.

name: Replay RCA
description: AI integrates multiple behavioural data streams - session replays, product analytics, in-product surveys, and experiment results - and generates diagnostic summaries that identify friction points, drop-off patterns, and behavioural anomalies. The value is in cross-source synthesis: rather than reviewing replays, dashboards, and survey results separately, AI connects signals across sources to produce a unified picture of where users struggle and why. Approver arrangement: AI proposes diagnoses by linking behavioural patterns to potential causes; the designer or researcher reviews whether the proposed connections are genuine or spurious. The risk is proportional to the convenience - AI-generated summaries that compress hours of replay review into a paragraph are valuable only if the compression preserves the diagnostically important detail and does not invent causal narratives from correlations.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: interpreting why users behave the way they do, not just what they do; AI can detect a drop-off pattern but cannot reliably distinguish confusion from abandonment from distraction without human judgment about user context and motivation
evaluating whether AI-generated cross-source connections are statistically meaningful or artefacts of small samples, selection bias, or confounding variables

value_hypothesis.sentence: Connects session replay, product analytics, survey responses, and experiment results into a unified diagnostic view, with AI generating summaries that highlight friction points and behavioural patterns across data sources.

value_indicator.description: elapsed time from behavioural anomaly detection to root-cause hypothesis, relative to manual multi-source review
proportion of AI-proposed friction points that, on investigation, represent genuine usability issues versus statistical noise or misinterpretation

risk_typology.narrative: AI may construct plausible causal narratives from correlational data: a drop-off coinciding with a UI change presented as caused by that change, when the actual driver is external (seasonal, marketing, competitor action); the narrative reads as diagnosis but is speculation

AI-generated diagnostic summaries compress the reasoning path from raw data to conclusion, making it difficult for designers to audit whether the diagnosis is based on representative evidence or cherry-picked patterns

designers who rely on AI-generated behavioural summaries may lose the practice of watching session replays and reading raw survey responses, where unexpected observations often produce the most valuable insights

attestation.diagnostic: N/A

name: Self-Healing DS Infrastructure
description: AI moves beyond monitoring (IMP-061) into autonomous maintenance of design system infrastructure. Where IMP-061 detects violations and reports them, this UC closes the loop: AI generates fixes for detected issues, updates documentation to reflect changes, and maintains token-to-code consistency without requiring human action for routine cases - the system not only knows what is wrong but acts to correct it. Auditor arrangement: AI operates independently on routine maintenance tasks (updating a deprecated token reference, correcting a spacing violation, regenerating documentation after a component change); the design system maintainer monitors outcomes and intervenes for cases requiring judgment - structural changes, ambiguous violations, or corrections affecting multiple dependent components. The distinction from DEL-055 (AI- Ready DS Architecture) is operational: DEL-055 builds the metadata, indexing and reasoning infrastructure that makes this autonomy possible; this UC is the infrastructure operating at scale over time.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: defining the boundary between routine maintenance (safe to automate) and structural decisions (requiring human judgment); the system must know when a token update is a simple propagation versus when it changes the semantic meaning of a design decision

ensuring that AI-generated fixes are implementable: that updated tokens resolve correctly across platforms, that documentation changes are accurate, and that automated corrections do not introduce cascading breakage

value_hypothesis.sentence: Extends design system

value_indicator.description: proportion of detected violations resolved by AI without human intervention, for cases within the defined routine-maintenance boundary
proportion of AI-generated corrections that are accepted without modification on human review, indicating reliable autonomous operation

risk_typology.narrative: AI-generated fixes may resolve the flagged violation while introducing subtler problems: a token replacement that satisfies the linting rule but breaks visual coherence in context, or a documentation update that is technically correct but misleading about component intent

autonomous corrections may work in isolation but fail at scale: a fix applied to one component may not account for dependencies, platform variations, or downstream consumers that rely on the pre-correction behaviour

when AI autonomously modifies design system infrastructure, the reasoning behind each change must be logged and auditable; without this, the system accumulates changes that no human can explain or reverse confidently

attestation.diagnostic: Tool gap - current design system tooling supports detection and reporting but autonomous fix generation and application at production scale requires tighter integration between design system metadata, code repositories, and AI agents than existing tools provide.

name: DS Usage Tracking
description: AI generates usage reports that measure how the design system is actually used in production - not whether implementations are correct (that is IMP-061, compliance monitoring), but whether the system is used at all. The reports track component utilization rates, identify shadow implementations (teams building custom versions of components that already exist in the system), flag unused components that may be candidates for deprecation, and measure usage trends across teams and releases. The Auditor arrangement reflects autonomous operation: AI scans codebases and products on schedule, generates structured usage reports, and flags anomalies (sudden drops in utilization, new shadow implementations, components with zero usage after launch). The design system maintainer reviews reports to determine action: whether low usage reflects a component quality problem, a discoverability problem, or a legitimate team need that the system does not yet serve. This UC makes the design system maintainer's case for investment evidence-based rather than anecdotal.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: interpreting usage data in context: low utilization of a component may mean it is poorly designed, poorly documented, hard to discover, or simply not needed; the expertise is in reading the data as a diagnostic signal rather than a scorecard

translating usage metrics into investment arguments: demonstrating the cost of shadow implementations, the value of consolidation, and the ROI of design system work in terms that leadership can act on

value_hypothesis.sentence: Automatically measures how the design system is actually

value_indicator.description: whether the team has quantitative data on component utilization, shadow implementation prevalence, and usage trends where previously they had only anecdotal evidence
measured decrease in custom re-implementations of existing design system components over time

risk_typology.narrative: usage dashboards create the appearance of design system health management when the metrics may be misleading: high utilization does not mean correct utilization, and low utilization does not mean the component is bad; teams may optimise for dashboard metrics rather than actual system quality

pressure to increase usage numbers may discourage legitimate contextual variation; teams that need a custom solution may default to the system component to avoid showing up as non-compliant in usage reports

when AI identifies a shadow implementation, the classification depends on how "shadow" is defined; design system maintainers need to understand the detection logic to avoid false positives that erode trust in the reporting

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - automated usage measurement requires structured design system metadata and codebase scanning infrastructure that most organisations have not yet built; manual usage tracking remains the norm.

name: Usability Test Analysis
description: After usability sessions are complete, AI processes recordings, transcripts, or observation notes to identify behavioural patterns, surface recurring issues, and generate a structured findings summary. The researcher reviews AI-generated themes and highlights, validates which observations are substantive, discards misattributions, and synthesises the approved findings into a research report. The Approver arrangement reflects the division of labour: AI performs the mechanical work of scanning and pattern-matching across session data at speed; the researcher gates what enters the findings.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: evaluating whether AI-identified patterns reflect genuine usability problems vs. incidental behaviours; distinguishing signal from noise in AI-generated theme summaries; and ensuring findings connect to the research questions that motivated the study
interpreting what observed behaviours reveal about user mental models and task strategies, rather than accepting AI surface-level descriptions of what participants did

value_hypothesis.sentence: Compresses the time from completed usability sessions to actionable findings by automating pattern detection, theme extraction, and highlight identification across session recordings and transcripts.

value_indicator.description: elapsed time from session completion to draft findings report relative to manual analysis baseline
proportion of session data incorporated into findings relative to manual analysis baseline

risk_typology.narrative: AI-generated themes and patterns may appear well-evidenced while misrepresenting participant behaviour; plausible-sounding findings can pass researcher review without the scrutiny that manual analysis would apply, particularly when session volume is high and review time is compressed

AI pattern detection reflects the distribution of observed behaviours, amplifying majority patterns and systematically underweighting edge cases, outliers, and the experiences of participants who deviate from the modal user
habitual delegation of pattern detection to AI may degrade the researcher's own observational acuity and capacity to notice the unexpected detail that often carries the most diagnostic value

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - AI-assisted session analysis is available in dedicated research platforms and widely trialled, but integration into formal research workflows - including standards for AI output review, confidence thresholds, and audit trails - has not been established.

name: Research Highlight Reels
description: AI reviews session recordings or transcripts and identifies moments of significant user behaviour - hesitations, errors, think-aloud commentary, task failures - then generates timestamped clips or quote extracts as a shareable highlight reel.
