The researcher reviews the proposed highlights, selects which clips accurately represent the findings, removes misleading or out-of-context moments, and shares the approved reel with stakeholders. The Approver arrangement reflects the workflow: AI proposes the selection; the researcher controls what is shared.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: evaluating whether AI-selected moments are genuinely representative of the finding being communicated, or whether they are vivid but atypical; ensuring the reel tells an honest story rather than a compelling one
sequencing and framing clips for stakeholder audiences who will draw design conclusions from them; the reel is a persuasion artifact as much as an evidence artifact

value_hypothesis.sentence: Compresses the time from raw session recordings to shareable evidence clips, enabling same-day stakeholder communication of usability findings without manual clip selection and editing.

value_indicator.description: elapsed time from session completion to shareable highlight reel relative to manual clip selection baseline
proportion of AI-selected highlights validated as accurately representative by the researcher

risk_typology.narrative: AI-selected highlights may be vivid and emotionally resonant while misrepresenting the overall pattern of findings; a reel of compelling moments can create a false picture of frequency or severity that stakeholders carry into design decisions

AI highlight selection favours observable, easily transcribed moments - verbal reactions, visible errors - and systematically underselects quiet difficulties, subtle hesitations, and non-verbal signals that experienced researchers would flag
a polished, same-day highlight reel may substitute for deeper analysis; stakeholders who have seen the reel may consider the research findings settled before full synthesis is complete

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - AI highlight generation is available in research platforms and demonstrably saves time, but protocols for researcher review, representativeness checks, and stakeholder framing have not been standardised.

name: Accessibility Audit
description: AI scans design files or live interfaces against WCAG criteria - checking colour contrast, focus order, alt text, heading structure, touch target size, and component labelling - and generates a structured report of identified violations. The designer or accessibility specialist reviews AI findings, validates which flags represent genuine issues, dismisses false positives, and prioritises remediation. More advanced implementations apply automated scanning within CI/CD pipelines, surfacing regressions at the point of code commit. Approver arrangement: AI executes the scan and surfaces candidates; the specialist gates what counts as a real issue and owns the fix decisions.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: understanding which AI-flagged issues represent genuine barriers for users with disabilities vs. technical violations with minimal real-world impact; prioritising remediation based on actual user harm rather than violation count
evaluating whether AI-proposed fixes preserve intended interaction patterns and do not introduce new usability problems while resolving accessibility violations

value_hypothesis.sentence: Increases the proportion of accessibility issues caught before development by automating WCAG compliance checks across designs and live interfaces, reducing the cost of remediation and compliance exposure.

value_indicator.description: proportion of WCAG criteria automatically checked relative to manual audit baseline
reduction in accessibility issues reaching development relative to pre-audit baseline

risk_typology.narrative: automated scans cover a subset of WCAG criteria reliably but cannot assess cognitive load, plain language, or contextual usability for screen reader users; a clean scan result may create false confidence that a design is accessible when significant barriers remain undetected

resolving AI-flagged violations may be mistaken for achieving accessibility; teams that address automated findings without user testing with disabled participants may ship designs that pass tools but fail people

accessibility models trained primarily on dominant-language, Western interface patterns may underperform on designs that serve non-standard languages, scripts, or culturally distinct interaction conventions

attestation.diagnostic: N/A

name: Synthetic Stakeholder Critique
description: AI adopts separate stakeholder personas - such as a business owner, accessibility advocate, or technical implementer - and delivers structured critique of a design from each perspective. Research evidence indicates that separate role orchestration, where each persona is maintained distinctly, produces higher designer trust and stronger creative support than a unified model delivering blended feedback. The designer engages with the critique, rebutting AI challenges, accepting actionable points, and deciding which perspectives reveal genuine gaps. More advanced implementations enable in-context remediation, allowing the designer to adjust the design in direct response to AI challenges without leaving the design environment. The Challenger arrangement reflects the interaction: AI generates critique the designer must actively respond to, rather than simply approve or discard.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: evaluating whether AI-simulated stakeholder perspectives accurately represent the concerns of real users and stakeholders, or whether they are plausible constructs that substitute for genuine constituency input; knowing when synthetic critique is sufficient and when real stakeholder engagement is required
distinguishing which AI-generated critiques reflect real design barriers from those that are technically coherent but contextually irrelevant to the specific audience and use case

value_hypothesis.sentence: Expands the range of stakeholder perspectives available during design review by simulating distinct critic roles at speed, surfacing objections the design team may not have anticipated before live stakeholder engagement.

value_indicator.description: proportion of AI-generated critique points that surface issues not identified through team review alone
reduction in rounds of stakeholder review required before design sign-off relative to unassisted baseline

risk_typology.narrative: AI stakeholder personas simulate perspective without genuine constituency; a design that passes synthetic critique may still fail real users and stakeholders whose actual concerns differ from what the model constructs, creating false confidence before live review

AI-generated critique may converge on the same categories of objection across projects, producing a predictable critique profile that misses the distinctive concerns of specific audiences or client contexts
substituting synthetic critique for facilitated stakeholder review may reduce the team's capacity to elicit, interpret, and act on genuine stakeholder input over time

attestation.diagnostic: Tool gap - controlled research (n=48 across two studies) demonstrates that separate-role AI critique improves designer trust and creative support relative to unified-model critique, but implementations remain research prototypes; no production-ready tool currently delivers multi-role stakeholder critique with in-context design remediation at scale.

name: Research Bias Audit
description: After completing synthesis from usability sessions or research data, the researcher prompts AI to audit the conclusions for methodological gaps - querying which participant segments may be underweighted, which hypotheses were not tested, and where the synthesis may overfit to the most vocal or visible participants. The researcher responds to each AI challenge, accepting those that reveal genuine blind spots and rebutting those that misread the research design. Practitioner evidence documents this as a formal post-synthesis step, with prompts such as "which segments might this synthesis underweight?" used to surface representation gaps before findings are shared. The Challenger arrangement reflects the structure: AI interrogates the researcher's conclusions; the researcher adjudicates which challenges hold.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: distinguishing AI-identified gaps that reveal genuine methodological weaknesses from those that reflect model unfamiliarity with the study's scope and design decisions; knowing which challenges to accept and which to rebut
evaluating whose perspectives are structurally absent from the synthesis and whether those absences materially affect the design decisions at stake

value_hypothesis.sentence: AI actively challenges the researcher's synthesis by probing for underrepresented segments, untested hypotheses, and interpretive overreach before findings are acted on.

value_indicator.description: proportion of AI-identified gaps confirmed by the researcher as genuine methodological weaknesses relative to unassisted baseline
proportion of synthesis-driven design recommendations revised following AI challenge before findings are shared

risk_typology.narrative: AI bias detection is itself partial; the model identifies representation gaps in structured data but cannot surface the interpretive framings and language choices that embed the researcher's own perspective into the synthesis narrative
completing an AI bias audit may signal research rigour without delivering it; a clean audit covers detectable gaps, not the deeper analytical assumptions that shape how findings are framed
habitual delegation of bias auditing to AI may reduce the researcher's own capacity to recognise and name blind spots in real time, particularly in settings where AI is unavailable or inappropriate

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - practitioner evidence documents post-synthesis AI bias auditing as an established technique, with specific prompt patterns in active use, but no standardised protocol, prompt library, or quality threshold for researcher response has been formalised across teams or organisations.

name: Predictive Attention Analysis
description: AI generates predicted attention heatmaps for design layouts, estimating where users are likely to look first, how long they will dwell on elements, and whether key components fall within natural scan paths. Predictions are produced by computational models trained on eye-tracking datasets rather than live user observation. The designer reviews the generated heatmap, assesses whether the predicted attention pattern supports the intended visual hierarchy, and makes a threshold decision: accept the AI prediction as sufficient evidence for the current decision, or commission live eye-tracking where the stakes are higher. Evidence positions the technique as an early screening tool - useful for catching hierarchy problems quickly - while recommending live eye-tracking where the design is novel, the audience is specific, or the decision is high-stakes. The Approver arrangement reflects this gate: AI produces the prediction; the designer determines whether it is sufficient.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: interpreting whether AI-predicted attention patterns support or undermine intended visual hierarchy and information flow; distinguishing a genuine design problem from model noise or prediction artefacts
calibrating when AI attention predictions provide sufficient evidence vs. when live eye-tracking is required given the stakes, novelty, and audience specificity of the design decision

value_hypothesis.sentence: AI generates predicted attention heatmaps in seconds with zero participants, enabling rapid visual hierarchy checks before committing to live eye-tracking sessions.

value_indicator.description: proportion of visual hierarchy decisions resolved via AI heatmaps without requiring live eye-tracking sessions
correlation between AI-predicted and observed attention patterns relative to eye-tracking baseline (ROC-AUC 0.75–0.84 in current evidence)

risk_typology.narrative: AI attention predictions are probabilistic estimates from training data and may not reflect actual scan paths for specific user populations, task contexts, or novel interface patterns; a strong average accuracy score leaves meaningful variance unaccounted for at the level of individual design decisions
routine acceptance of AI heatmaps may displace live eye-tracking for decisions that genuinely require user attention data, treating computational prediction as equivalent to observation
models trained on specific device types, screen sizes, or user populations may produce unreliable predictions for designs serving non-standard contexts, scripts, or audiences

attestation.diagnostic: N/A

name: Simulated Usability Testing
description: AI agents interact with a prototype or live interface, simulating user task attempts across a defined scenario set. Implementations range from tools that model individual user journeys to systems that generate thousands of simulated sessions in parallel, producing task completion rates, error patterns, and drop-off points. The researcher reviews agent-generated findings, identifies which behavioural patterns are plausible proxies for real user behaviour, and decides which issues warrant follow-up with live participants. The Approver arrangement reflects the gatekeeping role: AI produces the simulation output; the researcher determines what to act on and what requires real-world validation.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: evaluating which AI-simulated behaviours are credible proxies for real user responses and which reflect model assumptions about how users approach tasks, not how they actually do
interpreting task failure patterns in terms of underlying user mental models, rather than treating agent errors as equivalent to human errors

value_hypothesis.sentence: AI agents simulate user interactions with a prototype at scale, generating behavioural data and task completion patterns without recruiting participants or scheduling sessions.

value_indicator.description: time from prototype to behavioural findings relative to recruited usability testing baseline
proportion of AI-identified issues confirmed in subsequent live testing

risk_typology.narrative: AI agents simulate task completion but cannot replicate the emotional state, cognitive load, confusion, or lived context that shape how real users experience a design; simulated findings may be structurally plausible but humanly wrong
high-volume simulation output creates an appearance of confidence; large numbers of simulated sessions do not compensate for the gap between agent behaviour and human behaviour

teams that act on simulated findings without live validation may ship designs that pass agent testing but fail real users, treating simulation as a substitute for observation rather than a supplement to it

attestation.diagnostic: Tool gap - AI agent simulation tools are commercially available and actively marketed, but independent evidence on how well simulated findings predict real user behaviour has not been established.

name: Test Plan Drafting
description: Before conducting usability testing or other validation activities, the researcher provides a brief describing the design, the target user, and the evaluation objective. AI generates a draft evaluation plan suggesting appropriate methods, success metrics, sample criteria, and potential threats to the study's validity - such as task ordering effects or missing participant segments. The researcher reviews the draft, adjusts methods to fit real constraints, adds domain-specific criteria AI cannot anticipate, and finalises the plan. Evidence documents cases where AI identified methodological gaps the researcher had not considered, including missing counterbalancing conditions in multi-task studies. The Co-creator arrangement reflects the collaborative dynamic: AI provides a substantive draft; the researcher shapes it into a plan that fits the actual context.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: evaluating whether AI-suggested methods and metrics are appropriate for the specific design question, or whether they represent generic good practice that does not fit the study's actual constraints, timeline, and user population
anticipating how participant behaviour during a study may differ from model assumptions, and adjusting the evaluation design to capture the behaviours that actually matter

value_hypothesis.sentence: AI drafts a structured evaluation plan from a research brief, suggesting appropriate methods, metrics, and potential threats to validity that the researcher then refines and finalises.

value_indicator.description: elapsed time from research brief to complete evaluation plan relative to manual planning baseline
proportion of AI-identified methodological gaps confirmed as valid by the researcher

risk_typology.narrative: AI-generated evaluation plans may appear comprehensive while omitting study-specific constraints, ethical requirements, or recruitment challenges that a researcher would catch; a well-structured plan can reduce the scrutiny it receives
habitual use of AI to generate evaluation plans may reduce the researcher's own capacity to design studies from first principles, particularly for novel or non-standard research contexts
completing an AI-assisted plan may create a false sense of preparedness; the plan is only as good as the brief it is given, and AI cannot compensate for vague or incomplete research objectives

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - AI-assisted evaluation planning is in active practitioner use, with documented cases of AI identifying methodological gaps, but no standardised protocol for brief quality, plan review, or researcher override has been established.

name: Rich Interaction Testing
description: Design tools can demonstrate layout and navigation, but struggle to show richer behaviours in high fidelity - how data loads, how animations feel, or how interactions respond under real conditions. AI generates working code from a design brief or Figma layout in seconds, producing a prototype that behaves like the real product rather than simulating it. The designer assesses whether the generated behaviour accurately represents the intended interaction before exposing it to users or stakeholders - enabling feedback on complex, data-driven interactions long before a development sprint begins. The Approver arrangement reflects the designer's gatekeeping role: AI produces the functional prototype; the designer determines whether the behaviour is faithful enough to test.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: evaluating whether AI-generated behaviour accurately represents the intended interaction quality, or whether implementation shortcuts introduce experiences that would not appear in the final product
determining which interaction scenarios are worth testing functionally vs. which can be adequately evaluated through static or clickthrough methods

value_hypothesis.sentence: AI generates working prototypes that simulate real product behaviour - live data, animations, conditional states - enabling designers to test complex interactions without waiting for a development cycle.

value_indicator.description: time from interaction concept to testable functional prototype relative to development-dependent baseline
reduction in late-stage misalignment after early functional testing relative to static prototype baseline

risk_typology.narrative: AI-generated implementations may behave differently from the final production build; participants respond to what was generated, not what was designed, and findings may reflect prototype choices rather than genuine interaction problems
a prototype that behaves correctly in a sandbox may not be reproducible in the actual technology stack; interactions validated in testing may not ship as tested
successful functional testing may be read as design validation when it primarily confirms what the prototype does rather than what the final product will do

attestation.diagnostic: Workflow gap - AI-generated functional prototypes used as test stimuli are in active practitioner use, with evidence of richer participant engagement and faster iteration, but protocols for assessing prototype fidelity before testing and distinguishing prototype-specific from design-specific findings have not been standardised.

name: Heuristic Evaluation
description: AI analyses screenshots of a user interface against a set of established usability principles and produces a structured report of potential violations with explanations. The designer or evaluator reviews the findings, confirms which violations represent genuine usability problems, dismisses false positives, and prioritises issues for remediation. Evidence from controlled research shows AI identifies a higher proportion of violations than individual human evaluators, with particular strength on layout and visual consistency issues. AI performs less reliably on violations that span multiple screens or require understanding of how interface components behave in context. The Approver arrangement reflects the review process: AI executes the evaluation; the designer gates what enters the findings.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: evaluating whether AI-flagged violations represent genuine usability barriers in the context of the specific user task and product, or are technical violations with minimal real-world impact
prioritising remediation based on actual user impact rather than violation count, particularly for issues affecting users with specific access needs

value_hypothesis.sentence: AI analyses UI screenshots against established usability principles, producing a structured list of potential violations that a designer or specialist reviews and prioritises.

value_indicator.description: proportion of heuristic violations identified relative to specialist human evaluation baseline
time from design to findings report relative to manual evaluation baseline

risk_typology.narrative: AI reliably identifies visual and consistency violations but systematically misses cross-screen issues and violations requiring contextual understanding of the user's task; a comprehensive-looking report may still leave significant problems undetected
resolving AI-flagged violations may be mistaken for completing a usability evaluation; heuristic evaluation is not a substitute for testing with real users
habitual reliance on AI evaluation may reduce designers' own ability to apply usability principles through direct observation and reasoning

attestation.diagnostic: N/A

named_tools: [None specific - general LLM with screenshot input]

name: Embedded Evaluation
description: The designer describes the prototype and testing objectives directly inside the design tool. AI generates a test plan from the brief, participants complete the test, and findings - success metrics, AI- identified themes, confidence indicators, and video clips - return to the designer in the same environment. The designer reviews AI-generated findings, validates which themes accurately represent participant behaviour, and acts on results without leaving the design context. Approver arrangement: AI manages test execution and initial analysis; the designer reviews and decides what to act on.

expertise_differentiator.narrative: evaluating whether AI-generated themes accurately represent the pattern of participant behaviour, or whether the in-tool format compresses findings in ways that lose important nuance
interpreting test findings in relation to design intent and making revision decisions that address identified issues without introducing new ones

value_hypothesis.sentence: The full validation loop - test plan, participant sessions, and findings - runs inside the design tool, removing the need to switch between research and design environments.

value_indicator.description: proportion of research-to-design iterations completed without leaving the design tool
elapsed time from session completion to first design revision relative to traditional research platform baseline

risk_typology.narrative: the convenience of in-tool testing may increase test volume while reducing analysis depth; rapid results that require little interpretation may substitute for more rigorous evaluation
AI-generated themes and confidence scores give findings an appearance of precision that may reduce the scrutiny a researcher would otherwise apply
reducing friction between testing and designing may also reduce the cognitive distance that helps researchers evaluate findings independently of their design investment

attestation.diagnostic: N/A
